abstract:
Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) deliver exceptional performance but
require significant computational resources, limiting their deployment on
mobile and edge devices. Smaller VLMs typically mirror design choices of larger
models, such as extensive image tokenization, leading to inefficient GPU memory
usage and constrained practicality for on-device applications.
We introduce SmolVLM, a series of compact multimodal models specifically
engineered for resource-efficient inference. We systematically explore
architectural configurations, tokenization strategies, and data curation
optimized for low computational overhead. Through this, we identify key design
choices that yield substantial performance gains on image and video tasks with
minimal memory footprints.
Our smallest model, SmolVLM-256M, uses less than 1GB GPU memory during
inference and outperforms the 300-times larger Idefics-80B model, despite an
18-month development gap. Our largest model, at 2.2B parameters, rivals
state-of-the-art VLMs consuming twice the GPU memory. SmolVLM models extend
beyond static images, demonstrating robust video comprehension capabilities.
Our results emphasize that strategic architectural optimizations, aggressive
yet efficient tokenization, and carefully curated training data significantly
enhance multimodal performance, facilitating practical, energy-efficient
deployments at significantly smaller scales.

keyInsights: Smaller language models benefit less from larger vision encoders while larger models gain more from powerful encoders
Image splitting enhances performance for vision tasks but video frame averaging degrades results
System prompts and media tokens significantly improve compact VLM performance
Excessive chain-of-thought data harms compact model performance
originalProblem: Large vision-language models (VLMs) require significant computational resources, limiting their deployment on mobile and edge devices
Simply scaling down existing architectures leads to inefficient small models with degraded performance
results: SmolVLM-256M achieves competitive performance while using <1GB GPU memory
SmolVLM-2.2B rivals state-of-the-art VLMs with significantly lower resource usage
Models demonstrate strong generalization to video tasks without architecture modifications
Successful deployment on consumer hardware enables practical on-device applications
solution: Systematically explore architectural choices and training strategies optimized for compact models
Balance parameter allocation between vision encoder and language model components
Employ aggressive visual token compression and learned positional tokens
Optimize context length and training data composition for small-scale models
paper_summary.summary: Hugging Face and Stanford researchers develop SmolVLM, a family of compact multimodal models that process both images and videos while requiring less than 1GB of GPU memory for the smallest 256M parameter variant, achieving competitive performance through optimized architecture design and training strategies that specifically target resource-constrained environments.

title: SmolVLM: Redefining small and efficient multimodal models

abstract:
The integration of long-context capabilities with visual understanding
unlocks unprecedented potential for Vision Language Models (VLMs). However, the
quadratic attention complexity during the pre-filling phase remains a
significant obstacle to real-world deployment. To overcome this limitation, we
introduce MMInference (Multimodality Million tokens Inference), a dynamic
sparse attention method that accelerates the prefilling stage for long-context
multi-modal inputs. First, our analysis reveals that the temporal and spatial
locality of video input leads to a unique sparse pattern, the Grid pattern.
Simultaneously, VLMs exhibit markedly different sparse distributions across
different modalities. We introduce a permutation-based method to leverage the
unique Grid pattern and handle modality boundary issues. By offline search the
optimal sparse patterns for each head, MMInference constructs the sparse
distribution dynamically based on the input. We also provide optimized GPU
kernels for efficient sparse computations. Notably, MMInference integrates
seamlessly into existing VLM pipelines without any model modifications or
fine-tuning. Experiments on multi-modal benchmarks-including Video QA,
Captioning, VisionNIAH, and Mixed-Modality NIAH-with state-of-the-art
long-context VLMs (LongVila, LlavaVideo, VideoChat-Flash, Qwen2.5-VL) show that
MMInference accelerates the pre-filling stage by up to 8.3x at 1M tokens while
maintaining accuracy. Our code is available at this https URL

keyInsights: Visual inputs in long-context VLMs exhibit unique "Grid patterns" in their attention matrices due to spatiotemporal locality, distinguishing them from text-only models.
Mixed-modality VLM inputs introduce clear "modality boundaries" in attention, where cross-modality patterns diverge significantly from intra-modality patterns, necessitating specialized processing.

Sparse indices, while not generalizable across different modalities, can effectively extrapolate within segments of the same modality, even when interleaved with other modalities, providing efficiency without accuracy loss.
originalProblem: Long-context Visual Language Models (VLMs) suffer from significant "Time-to-First-Token" latency during pre-filling due to the quadratic complexity of their attention mechanism.

Existing sparse attention methods, primarily designed for text-only LLMs, fail to effectively exploit the unique sparsity patterns in multi-modal inputs, such as spatiotemporal locality in vision or distinct modality boundaries.
The high computational cost prevents the practical deployment and interactive use of powerful long-context VLMs in applications like robotics, autonomous driving, and advanced video understanding.
results: MMInference achieved pre-filling acceleration of up to 8.3 times compared to FlashAttention-2 and 1.7 times compared to MInference at 1M token context length, maintaining accuracy nearly identical to full attention across VLM benchmarks.
On the Mixed-Modality Needle In A Haystack (MM-NIAH) benchmark, MMInference maintained a 91.3% recall rate, outperforming MInference (88.0%) and closely matching full attention (90.9%).

The introduced "Grid pattern" demonstrated a kernel-level speedup of 12 times compared to other sparse patterns, highlighting the efficiency gains from permutation-based transformations and optimized GPU kernels.
solution: MMInference introduces a dynamic, modality-aware permutation-based sparse attention method developed through a system-algorithm co-design approach for VLMs.

It incorporates a novel "Grid sparse attention" pattern to efficiently handle intra-modality visual inputs and "hybrid modality sparse attention" (Q-Boundary and 2D-Boundary heads) for mixed-modality sequences.

The framework utilizes an offline modality-aware sparse attention search algorithm to identify optimal patterns for each attention head, combined with optimized GPU kernels (Triton, FlashAttention-2, PIT) for practical efficiency without model retraining.
paper_summary.summary: MMInference, a dynamic sparse attention method, accelerates the pre-filling stage of long-context Visual Language Models (VLMs) by up to 8.3 times compared to FlashAttention-2 while preserving near full attention accuracy. It exploits unique "Grid patterns" in visual inputs and "modality boundaries" in mixed-modality sequences through modality-aware permutation sparse attention, addressing a critical bottleneck for VLM deployment.

title: MMInference: Accelerating Pre-filling for Long-Context VLMs via  Modality-Aware Permutation Sparse Attention

abstract:
Large models such as Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision Language Models
(VLMs) have transformed artificial intelligence, powering applications in
natural language processing, computer vision, and multimodal learning. However,
fully fine-tuning these models remains expensive, requiring extensive
computational resources, memory, and task-specific data. Parameter-Efficient
Fine-Tuning (PEFT) has emerged as a promising solution that allows adapting
large models to downstream tasks by updating only a small portion of
parameters. This survey presents a comprehensive overview of PEFT techniques,
focusing on their motivations, design principles, and effectiveness. We begin
by analyzing the resource and accessibility challenges posed by traditional
fine-tuning and highlight key issues, such as overfitting, catastrophic
forgetting, and parameter inefficiency. We then introduce a structured taxonomy
of PEFT methods -- grouped into additive, selective, reparameterized, hybrid,
and unified frameworks -- and systematically compare their mechanisms and
trade-offs. Beyond taxonomy, we explore the impact of PEFT across diverse
domains, including language, vision, and generative modeling, showing how these
techniques offer strong performance with lower resource costs. We also discuss
important open challenges in scalability, interpretability, and robustness, and
suggest future directions such as federated learning, domain adaptation, and
theoretical grounding. Our goal is to provide a unified understanding of PEFT
and its growing role in enabling practical, efficient, and sustainable use of
large models.

keyInsights: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods can be systematically categorized into five main families: additive, reparameterized, selective, hybrid, and unified approaches, each with distinct mechanisms and trade-offs.
Reparameterized methods like LoRA leverage low-rank decomposition to reduce trainable parameters by over 99% for models like GPT-3 while maintaining performance comparable to full fine-tuning.

The choice of PEFT method depends on specific task requirements, computational resources, and desired performance characteristics, with different methods being more effective for knowledge-intensive, structural, or generative tasks.
originalProblem: Traditional fine-tuning of large language and vision models demands substantial computational resources, memory, and storage, making it inaccessible for many researchers and organizations.
Full fine-tuning often leads to overfitting on small datasets, catastrophic forgetting of previously learned knowledge, and parameter inefficiency due to redundancy.
Storing and deploying multiple fully fine-tuned models for various tasks results in significant storage requirements and practical deployment challenges.
results: PEFT techniques have been successfully applied across language, vision, and multimodal domains, enabling efficient adaptation of large models to specialized tasks like fine-grained classification, object detection, and cross-modal understanding.

Specific PEFT methods, such as adapters and prompt tuning, have shown effectiveness in language tasks, while LoRA significantly reduces the number of trainable parameters in large models without sacrificing performance.
The survey highlights the practical impact of PEFT in broadening the adoption of powerful AI models by making fine-tuning more accessible and resource-efficient.
solution: The paper surveys Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) techniques that adapt large pre-trained models by updating only a fraction of their parameters or introducing a limited number of new ones.
PEFT methods are classified into additive (e.g., adapters, prompt tuning), reparameterized (e.g., LoRA), selective (e.g., BitFit), hybrid, and unified approaches.
These methods enable efficient model adaptation by reducing memory, computational overhead, and storage requirements while aiming to maintain performance comparable to full fine-tuning.
paper_summary.summary: This survey provides a structured overview of Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) techniques, examining their mechanisms, trade-offs, and applications across language, vision, and multimodal domains. It details how PEFT methods, such as LoRA and various adapter designs, enable the efficient adaptation of large models by updating only a small fraction of parameters, thereby addressing the resource and storage challenges of full fine-tuning.

title: PEFT A2Z: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning Survey for Large Language and  Vision Models

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized a wide range of domains such as natural language processing, computer vision, and multi-modal tasks due to their ability to comprehend context and perform logical reasoning. However, the computational and memory demands of LLMs, particularly during inference, pose significant challenges when scaling them to real-world, long-context, and real-time applications. Key-Value (KV) cache management has emerged as a critical optimization technique for accelerating LLM inference by reducing redundant computations and improving memory utilization. This survey provides a comprehensive overview of KV cache management strategies for LLM acceleration, categorizing them into token-level, model-level, and system-level optimizations. Token-level strategies include KV cache selection, budget allocation, merging, quantization, and low-rank decomposition, while model-level optimizations focus on architectural innovations and attention mechanisms to enhance KV reuse. System-level approaches address memory management, scheduling, and hardware-aware designs to improve efficiency across diverse computing environments. Additionally, the survey provides an overview of both text and multimodal datasets and benchmarks used to evaluate these strategies. By presenting detailed taxonomies and comparative analyses, this work aims to offer useful insights for researchers and practitioners to support the development of efficient and scalable KV cache management techniques, contributing to the practical deployment of LLMs in real-world applications. The curated paper list for KV cache management is in: \href{this https URL}{this https URL}.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: The Key-Value (KV) cache's linear memory scaling and quadratic attention complexity during auto-regressive generation represent fundamental bottlenecks for LLM inference.
KV cache optimization strategies can be systematically classified into token-level, model-level, and system-level categories, each offering distinct mechanisms and trade-offs.
A holistic understanding of the evaluation landscape, including long-context text and multi-modal benchmarks, is essential for rigorously assessing and comparing KV cache management techniques.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) incur immense computational and memory demands during inference, particularly with increasing sequence lengths.
The Key-Value (KV) cache, crucial for auto-regressive generation in Transformer models, scales linearly with sequence length, causing memory limits to be rapidly exceeded.
This memory and computational bottleneck prevents the widespread deployment of LLMs in real-world applications requiring long-context understanding or real-time responses.
results: Presented a comprehensive, three-level taxonomy that effectively organizes the diverse landscape of KV cache management techniques for LLM acceleration.

Offered detailed insights into specific optimization methods, such as selective caching ("attention sinks"), low-bit quantization with outlier redistribution, and system-level solutions like PagedAttention.
Outlined key future research directions, emphasizing the integration of multi-level optimizations, real-world deployments, and incorporating privacy/security considerations for KV cache management.
solution: Developed a detailed, three-tiered taxonomy categorizing KV cache optimization strategies into token-level, model-level, and system-level approaches.
Provided an in-depth analysis and comparative discussion of specific techniques within each category, highlighting their mechanisms, advantages, and trade-offs.
Compiled a comprehensive overview of long-context text and multi-modal datasets, alongside relevant evaluation metrics, for benchmarking KV cache management strategies.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from leading institutions in Hong Kong, China, and Singapore present a comprehensive survey categorizing Key-Value (KV) cache management strategies for accelerating Large Language Model inference into token-level, model-level, and system-level optimizations. The work offers a detailed analysis of existing techniques, their trade-offs, and an overview of relevant benchmarks to guide future research and practical deployments.

title: A Survey on Large Language Model Acceleration based on KV Cache Management

abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) integrate visual and textual information, enabling a wide range of applications such as image captioning and visual question answering, making them crucial for modern AI systems. However, their high computational demands pose challenges for real-time applications. This has led to a growing focus on developing efficient vision language models. In this survey, we review key techniques for optimizing VLMs on edge and resource-constrained devices. We also explore compact VLM architectures, frameworks and provide detailed insights into the performance-memory trade-offs of efficient VLMs. Furthermore, we establish a GitHub repository at this https URL to compile all surveyed papers, which we will actively update. Our objective is to foster deeper research in this area.

keyInsights: INT8 quantization offers a balanced approach to VLM optimization, providing significant memory and latency reductions with relatively modest accuracy drops compared to other methods.
The trade-off between accuracy and efficiency is highly dependent on the specific optimization technique and the VLM's architecture and sensitivity.
Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) techniques are crucial for adapting pre-trained VLMs to new tasks with minimal resource overhead.
originalProblem: State-of-the-art Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have vast parameter counts and high computational demands, making them impractical for real-time inference.
Their large memory footprints and inference latencies prevent deployment on resource-constrained edge devices such as IoT systems, mobile phones, and autonomous robots.
This creates a critical bottleneck for enabling ubiquitous, privacy-preserving, and real-time multimodal AI applications at the edge.
results: INT8 quantization consistently reduced VLM memory footprint by 43-57% and improved inference speed, with `blip-vqa-base` experiencing a modest 0.85-1.54% accuracy drop.

Pruning and low-rank approximation techniques demonstrated substantial memory and latency benefits, but often at the cost of higher accuracy degradation, with some models losing nearly all accuracy at aggressive compression levels.
The survey compiled a comprehensive list of lightweight VLM architectures (e.g., SmolVLM, MobileVLM) and frameworks (e.g., Hugging Face Optimum, TensorRT-LLM) designed for efficient edge deployment.
solution: A systematic survey was conducted to categorize and analyze techniques for optimizing Vision-Language Models across pre-deployment, efficient fine-tuning, and runtime phases.
The work reviews lightweight VLM architectures, frameworks, and privacy-preserving distributed VLM approaches like Federated and Split Learning.
An empirical analysis was performed to evaluate the accuracy, memory, and latency trade-offs of quantization, pruning, and low-rank approximation on VLM performance.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the Mobile Pervasive & Sensor Computing Lab at UMBC conducted a comprehensive survey and taxonomy of techniques for optimizing Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for deployment on edge and resource-constrained devices. The work systematically categorizes pre-deployment, efficient fine-tuning, and runtime strategies, along with an empirical analysis demonstrating that INT8 quantization offers a balanced approach to memory and latency reduction with moderate accuracy drops, while pruning and low-rank approximation can incur greater accuracy penalties.

title: A Survey on Efficient Vision-Language Models

abstract: In this study, we identify the inefficient attention phenomena in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), notably within prominent models like LLaVA-1.5, QwenVL-Chat and Video-LLaVA. We find out that the attention computation over visual tokens is of extreme inefficiency in the deep layers of popular LVLMs, suggesting a need for a sparser approach compared to textual data handling. To this end, we introduce FastV, a versatile plug-and-play method designed to optimize computational efficiency by learning adaptive attention patterns in early layers and pruning visual tokens in subsequent ones. Our evaluations demonstrate FastV's ability to dramatically reduce computational costs (e.g., a 45 reduction in FLOPs for LLaVA-1.5-13B) without sacrificing performance in a wide range of image and video understanding tasks. The computational efficiency and performance trade-off of FastV are highly customizable and pareto-efficient. It can compress the FLOPs of a 13B-parameter model to achieve a lower budget than that of a 7B-parameter model, while still maintaining superior performance. We believe FastV has practical values for deployment of LVLMs in edge devices and commercial models. Code is released at this https URL.

keyInsights: Visual tokens, despite comprising the majority of input tokens in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), receive strikingly lower attention scores in deeper layers compared to textual tokens, becoming increasingly redundant.

Critical visual information is primarily aggregated onto 'anchor' text tokens (e.g., system prompts) in early layers of the LLM backbone, making direct attention to raw visual tokens less crucial in subsequent layers.
Exploiting this identified 'inefficient visual attention phenomena' allows for substantial computational savings during LVLM inference without compromising model performance.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) incur significant computational costs during inference due to the massive number of visual tokens generated from high-resolution images or video frames.

The large input sequence length, exacerbated by visual tokens, leads to quadratic complexity in the self-attention mechanism, hindering widespread deployment, especially on resource-constrained devices.
Existing LLM-specific inference optimizations or static visual token reduction methods often lead to substantial performance degradation when applied to LVLMs.
results: FastV achieves approximately 45% FLOPs reduction for LLaVA-1.5 and QwenVL-Chat models with nearly no performance loss across various image understanding benchmarks.

A LLaVA-1.5-13B model with FastV demonstrates lower inference latency than a vanilla LLaVA-1.5-7B while maintaining superior performance, enabling the deployment of larger models at the cost of smaller ones.

The method generalizes to video understanding (VideoLLaVA) where it reduces FLOPs and can improve video question answering performance, and it applies well to other LVLMs like InstructBLIP across a wider range of benchmarks.
solution: FastV introduces a plug-and-play method that dynamically prunes redundant visual tokens during LVLM inference without requiring model retraining or fine-tuning.
It integrates an 'image token re-rank and filtering module' that, after a specified layer (K), ranks visual tokens by their attention scores and discards a percentage (R%) of the least attended ones.
Subsequent layers process only the remaining visual tokens, leading to computational savings in both self-attention and Feed-Forward Network modules.
paper_summary.summary: FastV introduces a plug-and-play method for Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) that optimizes inference efficiency by dynamically pruning redundant visual tokens in deeper layers based on attention scores. The approach achieves approximately 45% FLOPs reduction while consistently maintaining or enhancing performance across various image and video understanding tasks.

title: An Image is Worth 1/2 Tokens After Layer 2: Plug-and-Play Inference Acceleration for Large Vision-Language Models

abstract:
Visual language models (VLMs) have made significant advances in accuracy in
recent years. However, their efficiency has received much less attention. This
paper introduces NVILA, a family of open VLMs designed to optimize both
efficiency and accuracy. Building on top of VILA, we improve its model
architecture by first scaling up the spatial and temporal resolutions, and then
compressing visual tokens. This "scale-then-compress" approach enables NVILA to
efficiently process high-resolution images and long videos. We also conduct a
systematic investigation to enhance the efficiency of NVILA throughout its
entire lifecycle, from training and fine-tuning to deployment. NVILA matches or
surpasses the accuracy of many leading open and proprietary VLMs across a wide
range of image and video benchmarks. At the same time, it reduces training
costs by 4.5X, fine-tuning memory usage by 3.4X, pre-filling latency by
1.6-2.2X, and decoding latency by 1.2-2.8X. We will soon make our code and
models available to facilitate reproducibility.

keyInsights: A "scale-then-compress" paradigm, combining adaptive scaling with aggressive token reduction, effectively balances visual information capture and computational efficiency for high-resolution images and long videos.
Holistic optimization across the entire VLM lifecycle, from data selection and training to fine-tuning and deployment, is crucial for achieving substantial efficiency gains.
Fine-tuning specific sub-components (e.g., Layer Normalization layers of the vision encoder) can make VLM adaptation memory-efficient and accessible without sacrificing accuracy.
originalProblem: Current state-of-the-art Visual Language Models (VLMs) incur prohibitively high training costs, requiring hundreds of GPU days.
Fine-tuning VLMs for domain-specific applications is memory-intensive, often exceeding 64GB, which limits accessibility on standard hardware.
Deploying VLMs on resource-constrained edge devices or in real-time applications faces significant computational and memory challenges due to their large size.
results: NVILA matches or surpasses the accuracy of many leading open-source models and performs competitively with powerful proprietary models across diverse image and video benchmarks.
The models reduce VLM training costs by 1.9x to 5.1x and accelerate inference by 1.6x to 2.8x compared to baselines through quantization and optimized kernels.
NVILA enables VLM fine-tuning under 24 GB of GPU memory, making adaptation accessible on consumer-grade GPUs, and demonstrates real-time robotic navigation on a single laptop GPU.
solution: Introduces a "Scale-Then-Compress" architectural paradigm with Dynamic-S2 for images and temporal averaging for videos to efficiently handle high-resolution and long-context visual inputs.
Employs efficient training techniques including unsupervised dataset pruning (DeltaLoss) to reduce data redundancy and FP8 mixed precision training for higher throughput.

Proposes optimized fine-tuning strategies with differentiated learning rates and efficient Layer Normalization (LN) tuning for the vision encoder, along with specialized inference engine optimizations including W8A8 and W4A16 quantization.
paper_summary.summary: NVILA, developed by a collaboration including NVIDIA and MIT, introduces a family of open Visual Language Models that achieve state-of-the-art accuracy on diverse image and video benchmarks while drastically improving efficiency across training, fine-tuning, and deployment. The models demonstrate training cost reductions of 1.9-5.1x and inference speedups of up to 2.8x, enabling practical deployment on resource-constrained devices and democratizing VLM development.

title: NVILA: Efficient Frontier Visual Language Models

abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have made remarkable strides, largely driven by their ability to process increasingly long and complex contexts, such as high-resolution images, extended video sequences, and lengthy audio input. While this ability significantly enhances MLLM capabilities, it introduces substantial computational challenges, primarily due to the quadratic complexity of self-attention mechanisms with numerous input tokens. To mitigate these bottlenecks, token compression has emerged as an auspicious and critical approach, efficiently reducing the number of tokens during both training and inference. In this paper, we present the first systematic survey and synthesis of the burgeoning field of multimodal long context token compression. Recognizing that effective compression strategies are deeply tied to the unique characteristics and redundancies of each modality, we categorize existing approaches by their primary data focus, enabling researchers to quickly access and learn methods tailored to their specific area of interest: (1) image-centric compression, which addresses spatial redundancy in visual data; (2) video-centric compression, which tackles spatio-temporal redundancy in dynamic sequences; and (3) audio-centric compression, which handles temporal and spectral redundancy in acoustic signals. Beyond this modality-driven categorization, we further dissect methods based on their underlying mechanisms, including transformation-based, similarity-based, attention-based, and query-based approaches. By providing a comprehensive and structured overview, this survey aims to consolidate current progress, identify key challenges, and inspire future research directions in this rapidly evolving domain.

keyInsights: Pervasive and substantial redundancy exists across all multimodal inputs, presenting a significant opportunity for computational optimization in MLLMs.

Token compression is not solely an efficiency enhancement but also intrinsically improves MLLMs by facilitating better modality alignment, enhancing information representation, and enabling "one-shot" long-context understanding.

Different compression mechanisms offer distinct trade-offs in terms of compression ratio flexibility, interpretability, and compatibility with optimized acceleration libraries, necessitating a nuanced approach to method selection.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) face severe computational challenges due to the quadratic scaling of the self-attention mechanism with increasing input token length.

Multimodal inputs like high-resolution images, long videos, and audio generate token sequences orders of magnitude longer than text, with multimodal tokens often constituting over 80% of the total sequence.

A significant portion (over 50%) of these multimodal tokens are redundant, receiving minimal attention, yet there was no systematic classification of MLLM-specific token compression methods to leverage this redundancy.
results: Image LLMs (e.g., LLaVA-1.5-7B) can achieve near-lossless performance on benchmarks like VQA2 and MM-Vet, even when aggressively compressing tokens to 10-25% of the original count (e.g., 64-128 tokens from 576).
Video LLMs demonstrate similar trends, with methods like HoliTom maintaining high performance on benchmarks like VideoMME while retaining 10-25% of the original video tokens.

The paper notes that achieving lossless compression becomes more challenging for larger, more capable models (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL), suggesting their increased sensitivity to information loss from compression.
solution: The authors provide the first systematic survey and comprehensive synthesis of multimodal long-context token compression techniques, establishing a clear definition for token compression in MLLMs.

They propose a dual-layered taxonomy, categorizing existing methods first by their dominant data modality (image-centric, video-centric, audio-centric) and then by their underlying algorithmic mechanisms (transformation-based, similarity-based, attention-based, and query-based approaches).
The survey discusses the synergy with other efficiency techniques, identifies key challenges faced by current methods, and proposes future research directions for the field.
paper_summary.summary: This paper presents the first systematic survey and taxonomy of token compression techniques for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), addressing their computational inefficiency with long multimodal contexts. The survey categorizes methods by modality and underlying mechanism, demonstrating that compression can reduce token counts to 10-25% of original input while maintaining near-lossless performance on various benchmarks for image and video MLLMs.

title: A Survey of Token Compression for Efficient Multimodal Large Language Models

abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have attracted extensive attention due to their
remarkable performance across various tasks. However, the substantial
computational and memory requirements of LLM inference pose challenges for
deployment in resource-constrained scenarios. Efforts within the field have
been directed towards developing techniques aimed at enhancing the efficiency
of LLM inference. This paper presents a comprehensive survey of the existing
literature on efficient LLM inference. We start by analyzing the primary causes
of the inefficient LLM inference, i.e., the large model size, the
quadratic-complexity attention operation, and the auto-regressive decoding
approach. Then, we introduce a comprehensive taxonomy that organizes the
current literature into data-level, model-level, and system-level optimization.
Moreover, the paper includes comparative experiments on representative methods
within critical sub-fields to provide quantitative insights. Last but not
least, we provide some knowledge summary and discuss future research
directions.

keyInsights: LLM inference inefficiency stems from distinct bottlenecks: large model size (memory/compute), quadratic attention (prefilling), and auto-regressive decoding (KV cache, memory access).

Optimizations can be systematically categorized into data-level (input/output), model-level (structure/compression), and system-level (engine/serving) approaches, each addressing specific inefficiencies.
Empirical evaluation of various techniques reveals their practical trade-offs; for instance, quantization benefits decoding speed while speculative decoding accelerates end-to-end generation.
originalProblem: Large Language Models face high computational and memory requirements during inference, leading to significant latency, low throughput, high power consumption, and large storage needs.

The primary causes of inefficiency are the large model size, the quadratic complexity of the attention operation in the prefilling stage, and the auto-regressive decoding approach with its increasing Key-Value (KV) cache.
These resource demands impede widespread deployment of LLMs, especially in resource-constrained environments and for large-scale cloud services.
results: Weight-only quantization accelerated the decoding stage of LLaMA-2 models by up to ~2.5x, primarily by reducing memory access costs, though it could increase prefilling latency.
Speculative decoding methods demonstrated substantial end-to-end speed-ups, with Eagle achieving 3.47-3.72x acceleration, and token tree verifiers proving highly effective.
LLM serving frameworks like vLLM and LightLLM significantly improved request throughput by employing paged KV cache memory management and continuous batching.
solution: The paper provides a comprehensive survey of existing literature on efficient LLM inference.
It proposes a novel, hierarchical taxonomy categorizing techniques into data-level, model-level, and system-level optimizations.
The work includes comparative experiments on representative methods, such as model quantization, speculative decoding, and LLM serving frameworks, to offer quantitative insights.
paper_summary.summary: This survey paper offers a comprehensive overview of techniques for optimizing the computational and memory demands of Large Language Model (LLM) inference. It introduces a tripartite taxonomy across data, model, and system levels, and provides quantitative comparisons of representative methods like quantization and speculative decoding to guide practitioners.

title: A Survey on Efficient Inference for Large Language Models

abstract:
The exponential growth of Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) has driven
advancements in cross-modal reasoning but at significant computational costs.
In this work, we focus on visual language models. We highlight the redundancy
and inefficiency in current vision encoders, and seek to construct an adaptive
compression method for multimodal data. In this work, we characterize a panoply
of visual token selection and merging approaches through both benchmarking and
qualitative analysis. In particular, we demonstrate that simple cluster-level
token aggregation outperforms prior state-of-the-art works in token selection
and merging, including merging at the vision encoder level and attention-based
approaches. We underline the redundancy in current vision encoders, and shed
light on several puzzling trends regarding principles of visual token selection
through cross-modal attention visualizations. This work is a first effort
towards more effective encoding and processing of high-dimensional data, and
paves the way for more scalable and sustainable multimodal systems.

keyInsights: Basic clustering and spatial sampling outperform complex attention-based methods
Visual attention scores don't strongly correlate with token importance
Saliency rankings are volatile and show little variance across different prompts
Current vision encoders contain significant redundant information
originalProblem: Vision-language models generate excessive redundant tokens, creating significant computational overhead
Current attention-based token selection methods are computationally expensive and may not correlate well with token importance
results: Cluster & Aggregate method consistently outperforms existing token selection techniques
Maintains model accuracy while reducing visual tokens by up to 75%
Simple random and spatial sampling achieve surprisingly competitive performance
Integration of clustering improves performance of basic saliency methods
solution: Training-free compression pipeline leveraging pre-LLM shared embedding space
Simple cluster-based token aggregation combined with spatial/random sampling
K-means++ clustering to group similar visual tokens before selection
paper_summary.summary: Stanford researchers demonstrate that simple clustering-based token compression methods outperform complex attention-based approaches for visual language models, achieving comparable accuracy while reducing visual tokens by up to 75% through cluster-level aggregation and spatial sampling techniques that leverage the shared embedding space between modalities.

title: Token Sequence Compression for Efficient Multimodal Computing

abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are increasingly tasked with ultra-long multimodal understanding. While linear architectures offer constant computation and memory footprints, they often struggle with high-frequency visual perception compared to standard Transformers. To bridge this gap, we introduce \textbf{InfiniteVL}. We first develop a hybrid base model called \textbf{InfiniteVL-Base} that interleaves a small fraction of Full Attention layers with Gated DeltaNet. Empowered by a tailored distillation and fine-tuning strategy, InfiniteVL-Base matches the fundamental multimodal performance of equivalent Transformers while achieving a \textbf{1.7$\times$} decoding speedup. However, the quadratic complexity of the retained Full Attention inevitably becomes an efficiency bottleneck when scaling to ultra long context. To break this barrier, we propose a novel Long-Sequence Architectural Fine-Tuning strategy that seamlessly transforms the dense attention into vision-specific sparse mechanisms. This yields two specialized variants: \textbf{InfiniteVL-Offline} for offline retrieval and \textbf{InfiniteVL-Online} for online streaming. By eliminating the computation explosion of global attention without sacrificing high-frequency visual recall, InfiniteVL-Offline achieves Transformer-level length generalization with a \textbf{5x} prefill acceleration at 256K context. Concurrently, InfiniteVL-Online delivers robust streaming perception with a constant memory footprint and a real-time throughput of \textbf{25} FPS. Code and models are available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

title: InfiniteVL: Synergizing Linear and Sparse Attention for Highly-Efficient, Unlimited-Input Vision-Language Models

abstract: Current multimodal large lanauge models possess strong perceptual and reasoning capabilities, however high computational and memory requirements make them difficult to deploy directly on on-device environments. While small-parameter models are progressively endowed with strong general capabilities, standard Vision Transformer (ViT) encoders remain a critical bottleneck, suffering from excessive latency and memory consumption when processing high-resolution this http URL address these challenges, we introduce HyperVL, an efficient multimodal large language model tailored for on-device inference. HyperVL adopts an image-tiling strategy to cap peak memory usage and incorporates two novel techniques: (1) a Visual Resolution Compressor (VRC) that adaptively predicts optimal encoding resolutions to eliminate redundant computation, and (2) Dual Consistency Learning (DCL), which aligns multi-scale ViT encoders within a unified framework, enabling dynamic switching between visual branches under a shared LLM. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HyperVL achieves state-of-the-art performance among models of comparable size across multiple benchmarks. Furthermore, it significantly significantly reduces latency and power consumption on real mobile devices, demonstrating its practicality for on-device multimodal inference.

keyInsights: Adaptive visual resolution compression, guided by image information density, can drastically reduce computational overhead and visual token count for MLLMs on edge devices with minimal performance impact.

A dual-branch training strategy with knowledge distillation (DCL) allows MLLMs to dynamically switch between different visual encoder complexities, offering a flexible balance between efficiency and accuracy for diverse on-device tasks.

Designing MLLMs with hardware characteristics in mind, such as serial processing to leverage NPU memory, is crucial for achieving superior linear scalability and constant memory footprint on resource-constrained edge platforms.
originalProblem: State-of-the-art multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are too computationally and memory-intensive for direct deployment on resource-constrained edge devices.

Vision Transformer (ViT) encoders, critical for visual processing in MLLMs, suffer from quadratic complexity, leading to excessive latency and memory consumption with high-resolution inputs common in on-device scenarios.
Existing methods to optimize on-device MLLMs often involve aggressive visual token compression, which can severely degrade model performance.
results: HyperVL achieved a 12.9x speedup in latency and a 6.8x reduction in peak memory overhead compared to Qwen3-VL-2B on a Qualcomm 8750 Platform, with linear scalability.

The 1.8B parameter HyperVL model scored an OpenCompass average of 64.5, outperforming several larger 2B-3B models, and excelled in internal Xiaomi benchmarks for image-text creation and user intent recognition.
HyperVL demonstrated strong robustness to W4A16 quantization, retaining over 98% of its FP16 performance on benchmarks like DocVQA, ChartQA, and OCRBench, confirming deployment readiness.
solution: Introduced HyperVL, an MLLM architecture combining a lightweight Visual Resolution Compressor (VRC) for adaptive image scaling, dual SigLIP2-based visual encoders (Base/Large), and a Qwen3 1.7B LLM.

Developed Dual Consistency Learning (DCL) to facilitate dynamic switching between the two visual encoders by alternately training them and distilling knowledge from the larger to the smaller branch, ensuring semantic alignment.

Implemented the Visual Resolution Compressor (VRC) as a plug-and-play module that dynamically predicts optimal image compression ratios, reducing visual token count and processing load based on image information density.
paper_summary.summary: The HyperAI Team at Xiaomi Corporation developed HyperVL, an efficient and dynamic multimodal large language model tailored for on-device inference on edge devices. It incorporates a Visual Resolution Compressor and Dual Consistency Learning, achieving a 12.9x speedup in latency and a 6.8x reduction in peak memory on mobile hardware compared to existing models, while maintaining competitive performance across various benchmarks.

title: HyperVL: An Efficient and Dynamic Multimodal Large Language Model for Edge Devices

abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) exhibit impressive multi-modal capabilities but suffer from prohibitive computational and memory demands, due to their long visual token sequences and massive parameter sizes. To address these issues, recent works have proposed training-free compression methods. However, existing efforts often suffer from three major limitations: (1) Current approaches do not decompose techniques into comparable modules, hindering fair evaluation across spatial and temporal redundancy. (2) Evaluation confined to simple single-turn tasks, failing to reflect performance in realistic scenarios. (3) Isolated use of individual compression techniques, without exploring their joint potential. To overcome these gaps, we introduce LLMC+, a comprehensive VLM compression benchmark with a versatile, plug-and-play toolkit. LLMC+ supports over 20 algorithms across five representative VLM families and enables systematic study of token-level and model-level compression. Our benchmark reveals that: (1) Spatial and temporal redundancies demand distinct technical strategies. (2) Token reduction methods degrade significantly in multi-turn dialogue and detail-sensitive tasks. (3) Combining token and model compression achieves extreme compression with minimal performance loss. We believe LLMC+ will facilitate fair evaluation and inspire future research in efficient VLM. Our code is available at this https URL.
title: LLMC+: Benchmarking Vision-Language Model Compression with a Plug-and-play Toolkit

abstract: Recent advancements in Large Visual Language Models (LVLMs) have gained significant attention due to their remarkable reasoning capabilities and proficiency in generalization. However, processing a large number of visual tokens and generating long-context outputs impose substantial computational overhead, leading to excessive demands for key-value (KV) cache. To address this critical bottleneck, we propose AirCache, a novel KV cache compression method aimed at accelerating LVLMs inference. This work systematically investigates the correlations between visual and textual tokens within the attention mechanisms of LVLMs. Our empirical analysis reveals considerable redundancy in cached visual tokens, wherein strategically eliminating these tokens preserves model performance while significantly accelerating context generation. Inspired by these findings, we introduce an elite observation window for assessing the importance of visual components in the KV cache, focusing on stable inter-modal relevancy modeling with enhanced multi-perspective consistency. Additionally, we develop an adaptive layer-wise budget allocation strategy that capitalizes on the strength and skewness of token importance distribution, showcasing superior efficiency compared to uniform allocation. Comprehensive evaluations across multiple LVLMs and benchmarks demonstrate that our method achieves comparable performance to the full cache while retaining only 10% of visual KV cache, thereby reducing decoding latency by 29% to 66% across various batch size and prompt length of inputs. Notably, as cache retention rates decrease, our method exhibits increasing performance advantages over existing approaches.
title: AirCache: Activating Inter-modal Relevancy KV Cache Compression for Efficient Large Vision-Language Model Inference

abstract: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) represent a significant frontier in embodied intelligence, aiming to bridge digital knowledge with physical-world interaction. Despite their remarkable performance, foundational VLAs are hindered by the prohibitive computational and data demands inherent to their large-scale architectures. While a surge of recent research has focused on enhancing VLA efficiency, the field lacks a unified framework to consolidate these disparate advancements. To bridge this gap, this survey presents the first comprehensive review of Efficient Vision-Language-Action models (Efficient VLAs) across the entire model-training-data pipeline. Specifically, we introduce a unified taxonomy to systematically organize the disparate efforts in this domain, categorizing current techniques into three core pillars: (1) Efficient Model Design, focusing on efficient architectures and model compression; (2) Efficient Training, which reduces computational burdens during model learning; and (3) Efficient Data Collection, which addresses the bottlenecks in acquiring and utilizing robotic data. Through a critical review of state-of-the-art methods within this framework, this survey not only establishes a foundational reference for the community but also summarizes representative applications, delineates key challenges, and charts a roadmap for future research. We maintain a continuously updated project page to track our latest developments: this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Efficiency is a fundamental prerequisite for VLA models to transition from prototypes to practical, deployable systems on resource-constrained edge devices.
A holistic approach addressing model architecture, training paradigms, and data acquisition strategies concurrently is essential for achieving substantial efficiency gains in VLAs.

Research efforts are converging on techniques like parameter-efficient fine-tuning, leveraging massively parallel simulation, and utilizing vast internet-scale human video data to overcome the inherent resource demands of powerful VLA models.
originalProblem: Foundational VLA models inherit prohibitive computational and data footprints from their LLM and VLM predecessors, hindering real-world deployment.
High inference latency and insufficient control frequency make VLAs incompatible for real-time robotic manipulation on edge devices.
Existing approaches require excessive pre-training costs and rely on inefficient, labor-intensive data collection, creating significant barriers to practical application.
results: Efficient Model Design leverages innovations such as linear-time attention, Mamba-based backbones, parallel action decoding, layer pruning, and quantization to reduce model size and inference costs (e.g., SARA-RT, RoboMamba, OpenVLA).

Efficient Training employs data-efficient pre-training (e.g., self-supervised learning on egocentric videos, mixed data co-training) and parameter-efficient post-training methods (e.g., LoRA) to minimize computational and data demands during learning.

Efficient Data Collection shifts from labor-intensive teleoperation to scalable methods including massively parallel simulation, utilization of internet-scale and cross-domain human data, and autonomous self-exploration, often augmented by LLM-driven semantic annotations.
solution: The authors conducted the first comprehensive survey specifically dedicated to efficient VLAs, covering the entire "model-training-data" process.
They introduced a novel, systematically structured taxonomy organizing core technical landscapes into three pillars: Efficient Model Design, Efficient Training, and Efficient Data Collection.
The survey critically distills key challenges and current limitations, outlining forward-looking research directions to guide future endeavors in scalable embodied intelligence.
paper_summary.summary: This survey paper provides the first comprehensive review of efficient Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, consolidating advancements in model design, training, and data collection to enable practical deployment on resource-constrained devices. It details methodologies that significantly reduce computational costs and data footprints, offering a structured framework and future roadmap for scalable embodied intelligence.

title: A Survey on Efficient Vision-Language-Action Models

abstract: Speculative decoding is a powerful way to accelerate autoregressive large language models (LLMs), but directly porting it to vision-language models (VLMs) faces unique systems constraints: the prefill stage is dominated by visual tokens whose count scales with image resolution and video length, inflating both compute and memory, especially the key-value (KV) cache. We study speculative decoding for VLMs and introduce SpecVLM, a practical system that (1) establishes a strong EAGLE-2-style baseline, EagleVLM, delivering 1.5--2.3x end-to-end speedups over full autoregressive inference, and (2) further accelerates VLM inference with an elastic visual compressor that adaptively selects among pruning, pooling, convolution, and resampler primitives to balance FLOPs/parameters and accuracy per input. To avoid costly offline distillation corpora, we propose an online-logit distillation protocol that trains the draft model with on-the-fly teacher logits and penultimate features using a combined cross-entropy and Smooth L1 objective, eliminating storage and preprocessing while remaining compute-efficient. This protocol reveals a training-time scaling effect: longer online training monotonically increases the draft model's average accepted length, improving speculative efficiency. Empirically, SpecVLM achieves additional acceleration, culminating in 2.5--2.9x end-to-end speedups within 5 epochs across LLaVA and MMMU, consistently over resolutions and task difficulties, while preserving the target model's output distribution (lossless decoding). Our code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: The VLM performance bottleneck is dual, stemming from both the prefill stage, heavily impacted by visual input processing, and the accumulated cost of next-token prediction.
Dynamically adapting visual token compression strategies is essential for VLM speculative decoding, optimizing compute-accuracy trade-offs across diverse visual inputs and tasks.
Online-logit distillation provides a viable and efficient method for training VLM draft models, improving draft-target alignment without the need for extensive offline distillation corpora.
Speculative decoding in VLMs exhibits a 'training-time scaling effect,' where prolonged online training monotonically increases the average accepted token length and overall inference speedup.
originalProblem: Autoregressive inference in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) faces significant computational and memory bottlenecks due to extensive visual inputs, particularly during the prefill stage and KV cache accumulation.
Direct application of speculative decoding from LLMs to VLMs is inefficient as it doesn't account for the unique challenges of visual token processing.
Training VLM draft models for speculative decoding is costly and storage-intensive due to the need for large offline distillation corpora.
results: SpecVLM achieved end-to-end speedups up to 2.9 times on LLaVA benchmarks and MMMU-V1, consistently outperforming baselines while preserving output fidelity, with average accepted tokens exceeding 3.8.
The elastic visual compressor's dynamic selection mode contributed to an average 2.03x speedup on LLaVA-1.6-7B, demonstrating its role in managing visual token processing for optimal efficiency.

Empirical evidence confirmed a 'training-time scaling effect,' where extended online distillation monotonically improved average accepted token length and inference speedup, with optimal gains observed around 5 epochs.
solution: Implemented SpecVLM, a framework that extends speculative decoding to VLMs, incorporating a lightweight draft model and improving training stability with input layer normalization.

Developed an elastic visual compressor that dynamically selects optimal compression techniques (pruning, pooling, convolution, resampler) for visual tokens based on input characteristics and task demands.
Introduced an online-logit distillation protocol, enabling efficient, on-the-fly training of the VLM draft model using target model's logits and features, without needing large precomputed datasets.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed SpecVLM, a framework that accelerates Vision-Language Model inference by up to 2.9 times. It uses an elastic visual compression component and an efficient online-logit distillation protocol for training its draft model, ensuring the target model's output distribution remains unchanged.

title: SpecVLM: Fast Speculative Decoding in Vision-Language Models

abstract:
Large multimodal models excel in multimodal tasks but face significant
computational challenges due to excessive computation on visual tokens. Unlike
token reduction methods that focus on token-level redundancy, we identify and
study the computation-level redundancy on vision tokens to ensure no
information loss. Our key insight is that vision tokens from the pretrained
vision encoder do not necessarily require all the heavy operations (e.g.,
self-attention, FFNs) in decoder-only LMMs and could be processed more lightly
with proper designs. We designed a series of experiments to discover and
progressively squeeze out the vision-related computation redundancy. Based on
our findings, we propose ProxyV, a novel approach that utilizes proxy vision
tokens to alleviate the computational burden on original vision tokens. ProxyV
enhances efficiency without compromising performance and can even yield notable
performance gains in scenarios with more moderate efficiency improvements.
Furthermore, the flexibility of ProxyV is demonstrated through its combination
with token reduction methods to boost efficiency further. The code will be made
public at this this https URL URL.

keyInsights: Computation-level redundancy exists in processing vision tokens within decoder-only LMMs, particularly in middle and later layers
Lightweight vision-specific modules can compensate for performance drops from skipping heavy operations and potentially improve overall performance
Preserving all original visual tokens while reducing computation maintains fine-grained details better than token reduction approaches
originalProblem: Large Multimodal Models require processing hundreds of visual tokens through computationally expensive self-attention and feed-forward operations, creating significant computational overhead
Existing token reduction methods risk discarding fine-grained visual details and contextual information, particularly in dense visual content scenarios
Cross-attention-based approaches require additional parameters and training complexity while moving away from simple decoder-only architectures
results: ProxyV achieves 101% performance with 43% reduction in prefilling FLOPs and 40% reduction in processing time on Vicuna1.5-7B
Non-spatial variant achieves 102.4% performance with 36% FLOPs reduction and 33% time reduction on fine-grained visual understanding benchmarks
Method demonstrates effectiveness across OCR-extensive tasks, general multimodal tasks, and document parsing while maintaining architectural simplicity
solution: Introduce ProxyV, which uses a small group of proxy vision tokens to represent original visual tokens during computationally intensive operations
Replace heavy attention and FFN operations on vision tokens with lightweight MLP modules in middle and later layers
Develop both spatial and non-spatial variants, with the latter enabling combination with existing token reduction methods
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Nanyang Technological University and SenseTime Research develop ProxyV, a method that reduces computational overhead in Large Multimodal Models by using a small set of proxy vision tokens to represent original visual tokens during expensive operations, achieving 43% reduction in prefilling FLOPs and 40% reduction in processing time while maintaining 101% performance on fine-grained visual understanding benchmarks.

title: Streamline Without Sacrifice -- Squeeze out Computation Redundancy in  LMM

abstract:
Vision Large Language Models (VLMs) combine visual understanding with natural
language processing, enabling tasks like image captioning, visual question
answering, and video analysis. While VLMs show impressive capabilities across
domains such as autonomous vehicles, smart surveillance, and healthcare, their
deployment on resource-constrained edge devices remains challenging due to
processing power, memory, and energy limitations. This survey explores recent
advancements in optimizing VLMs for edge environments, focusing on model
compression techniques, including pruning, quantization, knowledge
distillation, and specialized hardware solutions that enhance efficiency. We
provide a detailed discussion of efficient training and fine-tuning methods,
edge deployment challenges, and privacy considerations. Additionally, we
discuss the diverse applications of lightweight VLMs across healthcare,
environmental monitoring, and autonomous systems, illustrating their growing
impact. By highlighting key design strategies, current challenges, and offering
recommendations for future directions, this survey aims to inspire further
research into the practical deployment of VLMs, ultimately making advanced AI
accessible in resource-limited settings.

organization_info.name: Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence

keyInsights: Efficient fine-tuning methods like prompt-based tuning and adapter-based tuning are crucial for adapting large VLMs to edge tasks without extensive retraining.
Federated Learning is a key enabler for privacy-preserving, decentralized training and deployment of VLMs on heterogeneous edge devices.
Robust security and privacy mechanisms, including differential privacy and homomorphic encryption, are non-negotiable for deploying VLMs that handle sensitive data at the edge.
originalProblem: Modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are computationally intensive and have large parameter counts, making them impractical for deployment on resource-constrained edge devices.

The 'cloud-edge gap' restricts advanced AI capabilities to centralized computing environments, leading to high latency, increased bandwidth usage, and data privacy concerns for real-time edge applications.
Existing VLM surveys often focus on architectures or fine-tuning without holistically addressing the unique challenges, security, and privacy considerations of edge deployment.
results: Model compression techniques (quantization, pruning, knowledge distillation) are indispensable for reducing VLM size and computational demands, enabling their use on edge hardware.
A range of lightweight and edge-optimized VLMs (e.g., MobileVLM V2, Moondream2) have emerged, demonstrating significantly reduced parameter counts while maintaining competitive performance.
Lightweight VLMs facilitate broad applications in diverse domains, including healthcare, environmental monitoring, autonomous systems, and surveillance, by enabling real-time, on-device intelligence.
solution: The paper systematically surveys lightweight VLM architectures and efficient fine-tuning methods (prompt-based and adapter-based) to reduce computational and memory footprints for edge devices.
It details a comprehensive five-step design process for distributed edge VLM deployment, emphasizing data selection, model compression, and federated learning paradigms.
The survey identifies and categorizes critical open challenges, including secure and privacy-aware VLM deployment, context-aware VLMs, and cross-modality learning for multi-sensor edge environments.
paper_summary.summary: This survey from MBZUAI provides a comprehensive overview of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) tailored for edge networks, addressing the challenge of deploying large models on resource-constrained devices. It synthesizes advancements in lightweight VLM design, efficient fine-tuning, and distributed deployment strategies, while also detailing critical security, privacy, and communication considerations.

title: Vision-Language Models for Edge Networks: A Comprehensive Survey

abstract: Vision Language Models (VLMs) have rapidly advanced in integrating visual and textual reasoning, powering applications across high-resolution image understanding, long-video analysis, and multi-turn conversation. However, their scalability remains limited by the growing number of visual tokens that dominate inference latency. We present SparseVILA, a new paradigm for efficient VLM inference that decouples visual sparsity across the prefilling and decoding stages. SparseVILA distributes sparsity across stages by pruning redundant visual tokens during prefill and retrieving only query-relevant tokens during decoding. This decoupled design matches leading prefill pruning methods while preserving multi-turn fidelity by retaining most of the visual cache so that query-aware tokens can be retrieved at each conversation round. Built on an AWQ-optimized inference pipeline, SparseVILA achieves up to 4.0 times faster prefilling, 2.5 times faster decoding, and an overall 2.6 times end-to-end speedup on long-context video tasks -- while improving accuracy on document-understanding and reasoning tasks. By decoupling query-agnostic pruning and query-aware retrieval, SparseVILA establishes a new direction for efficient multimodal inference, offering a training-free, architecture-agnostic framework for accelerating large VLMs without sacrificing capability.

keyInsights: Visual sparsity should be applied in a decoupled manner, with coarse pruning in prefill and dynamic, query-aware retrieval in decoding, to match the distinct computational profiles of each stage.

Preserving the full visual context in the KV cache during prefill, even if not immediately used, is crucial for maintaining multi-turn conversational fidelity and preventing irreversible information loss.
Aggressively focusing computation on dynamically relevant visual tokens during the memory-bound decoding stage can lead to both speedups and improved accuracy by enhancing model focus.
originalProblem: Vision Language Models (VLMs) suffer from high inference latency and memory consumption due to the vast number of visual tokens processed by Large Language Models.
Existing query-aware pruning methods for VLMs cause irreversible information loss, leading to degraded performance in multi-turn conversations.
Optimizing VLM inference is challenging across its distinct stages: the compute-bound prefill phase and the memory-bound decoding phase.
results: Achieves up to 4.0x faster prefilling, 2.5x faster decoding, and an overall 2.6x end-to-end speedup on long-context video tasks, using an AWQ-optimized pipeline on an NVIDIA A6000 GPU.
Maintains near-lossless accuracy on general VQA tasks and significantly reduces degradation (over 15% less) on fine-grained document and chart understanding benchmarks compared to prior methods.
Demonstrates improved accuracy on video understanding, video captioning, and physical reasoning tasks, with up to a 4.5% performance gain over unpruned models, while delivering substantial speedups.
solution: SparseVILA implements a decoupled sparsity framework, performing lightweight, query-agnostic visual pruning during the prefill stage to reduce global redundancy.
During the decoding stage, it employs aggressive, query-aware retrieval, dynamically selecting only the most relevant visual tokens from the preserved KV cache for attention computation at each step.
Custom Triton kernels are used for efficient token salience estimation (prefill) and relevance computation (decode), significantly accelerating these operations.
paper_summary.summary: SparseVILA introduces a decoupled sparsity framework for Vision Language Models, applying query-agnostic pruning in prefill and query-aware retrieval during decoding to manage visual tokens. This approach accelerates VLM inference by up to 2.6x end-to-end on long-context video tasks and improves accuracy on certain video understanding and reasoning benchmarks.

title: SparseVILA: Decoupling Visual Sparsity for Efficient VLM Inference

abstract: Speculative decoding is a widely adopted technique for accelerating inference in large language models (LLMs), yet its application to vision-language models (VLMs) remains underexplored, with existing methods achieving only modest speedups (<1.5x). This gap is increasingly significant as multimodal capabilities become central to large-scale models. We hypothesize that large VLMs can effectively filter redundant image information layer by layer without compromising textual comprehension, whereas smaller draft models struggle to do so. To address this, we introduce Vision-Aware Speculative Decoding (ViSpec), a novel framework tailored for VLMs. ViSpec employs a lightweight vision adaptor module to compress image tokens into a compact representation, which is seamlessly integrated into the draft model's attention mechanism while preserving original image positional information. Additionally, we extract a global feature vector for each input image and augment all subsequent text tokens with this feature to enhance multimodal coherence. To overcome the scarcity of multimodal datasets with long assistant responses, we curate a specialized training dataset by repurposing existing datasets and generating extended outputs using the target VLM with modified prompts. Our training strategy mitigates the risk of the draft model exploiting direct access to the target model's hidden states, which could otherwise lead to shortcut learning when training solely on target model outputs. Extensive experiments validate ViSpec, achieving, to our knowledge, the first substantial speedup in VLM speculative decoding. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Effective VLM speculative decoding requires vision-aware mechanisms tailored to handle the unique characteristics and redundancy of visual data, rather than direct adaptation from text-only methods.
Compressing visual embeddings into a few salient tokens significantly improves the efficiency and predictive accuracy of shallow draft models.
Continuously injecting global visual context into the draft model's hidden states is crucial for maintaining multimodal alignment and mitigating context loss over extended generation.
Synthetic data generation through targeted prompting of target VLMs can effectively create large-scale, high-quality training data for VLM draft models, overcoming data scarcity.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) face significant inference time challenges due to their increasing architectural scale and multimodal complexity, hindering practical deployment.
Existing speculative decoding methods, effective for text-only LLMs, provide only modest speedups (<1.5x) for VLMs, struggling to efficiently process redundant visual information.
Shallow draft models in speculative decoding often overlook crucial visual context in long multimodal sequences, leading to a 'lost-in-the-middle' effect and reduced textual coherence.
results: ViSpec consistently outperforms baselines (Medusa, EAGLE-2) in speedup and average acceptance length across VLM architectures and eight multimodal tasks, achieving an average 2.58x speedup (τ=2.98) for LLaVA-v1.6-Vicuna-7B.

Ablation studies confirm the synergistic impact of ViSpec's components, with image embedding compression boosting speedup by up to 30%, global feature injection by ~7%, and dataset generation by an additional 30%.

The framework demonstrates robust performance across diverse tasks and maintains strong acceleration even for high-resolution images and shows potential for temporal data without specific video training.
solution: Employs a lightweight, Q-Former-inspired vision adaptor to compress high-dimensional, redundant image embeddings into a concise set of visual features for the draft model.
Integrates a global visual feature vector into the hidden states of all text tokens during generation, ensuring persistent visual context for the draft model in long sequences.
Generates a synthetic dataset of long, detailed multimodal responses by prompting target VLMs, addressing the scarcity of suitable training data for VLM speculative decoding.
paper_summary.summary: ViSpec introduces a vision-aware speculative decoding framework to accelerate vision-language model inference, achieving up to 3.22x speedup and an average 2.58x for LLaVA-v1.6-Vicuna-7B on multimodal tasks compared to standard decoding while maintaining output quality. The Huawei Noah's Ark Lab and Peking University research addresses specific challenges of visual data processing within speculative decoding.

title: ViSpec: Accelerating Vision-Language Models with Vision-Aware Speculative Decoding

abstract: In the past year, Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable performance in tasks such as visual question answering, visual understanding and reasoning. However, the extensive model size and high training and inference costs have hindered the widespread application of MLLMs in academia and industry. Thus, studying efficient and lightweight MLLMs has enormous potential, especially in edge computing scenarios. In this survey, we provide a comprehensive and systematic review of the current state of efficient MLLMs. Specifically, we summarize the timeline of representative efficient MLLMs, research state of efficient structures and strategies, and the applications. Finally, we discuss the limitations of current efficient MLLM research and promising future directions. Please refer to our GitHub repository for more details: this https URL.

keyInsights: Efficiency in MLLMs is achieved through a multi-faceted approach, combining architectural innovations, optimized training methodologies, and strategic data utilization, rather than solely relying on smaller base models.

Techniques like vision token compression, Mixture-of-Experts (MoE), and Mamba architectures are crucial for effectively processing high-resolution inputs and improving inference speed while maintaining scalability.

High-quality pre-training and instruction-tuning data, coupled with parameter-efficient transfer learning techniques, are essential for adapting MLLMs to specific tasks with reduced resource expenditure and preserving pre-trained knowledge.
originalProblem: Contemporary Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) achieve high performance due to scaling laws, but incur immense computational and memory costs for both training and inference.
The extensive resource demands restrict MLLM access to a few dominant corporations, raising concerns about the democratization of AI and user privacy.
Deploying powerful MLLMs on resource-constrained edge devices remains a significant challenge, hindering widespread adoption and real-world application.
results: Architectural advancements, including compact vision encoders, efficient vision-language projectors (e.g., Mamba-based), and Small Language Models (SLMs) under 3 billion parameters, are fundamental to constructing efficient MLLMs.
Vision token compression methods, such as multi-view input and specialized token processing modules, effectively manage the computational burden of high-resolution images and long video sequences.

Empirical comparisons show that many efficient MLLMs achieve performance comparable to, and occasionally surpass, much larger general MLLMs on 14 established visual-language benchmarks, enabling their practical use in resource-sensitive applications like biomedical analysis and document understanding.
solution: The authors conducted a systematic literature review, synthesizing and organizing existing research on efficient MLLMs.
A six-primary category taxonomy was developed to classify diverse research efforts: Architecture, Efficient Vision, Efficient LLMs, Training, Data and Benchmarks, and Application.
A detailed comparative analysis was performed on 22 efficient MLLMs and 13 larger MLLMs across 14 established visual-language benchmarks, providing empirical context and a public GitHub repository.
paper_summary.summary: A collaborative team from Tencent Youtu Lab, SJTU, BAAI, and ECNU presents a comprehensive survey categorizing and analyzing methods for developing efficient Multimodal Large Language Models. The survey demonstrates how diverse architectural and training strategies enable models with reduced computational costs to achieve performance competitive with, or sometimes exceeding, much larger general MLLMs across 14 visual-language benchmarks.

title: Efficient Multimodal Large Language Models: A Survey

abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in multimodal understanding tasks. However, the increasing demand for high-resolution image and long-video understanding results in substantial token counts, consequently leading to reduced inference efficiency. Token compression offers a direct solution by reducing the number of tokens to be processed, thereby improving computational efficiency without architectural changes. Through extensive analysis, we identify two critical limitations in existing inner-LLM token compression methods: positional bias and incompatibility with efficient operators, which critically hinder their practical deployment for LVLM acceleration. This paper presents the first approach from a dynamic token variation perspective, revealing that visual token variations within LLMs exhibit task-agnostic properties. We propose Variation-aware Vision Token Dropping (\textit{i.e.}, \textbf{V$^2$Drop}), which progressively removes visual tokens with minimal variation during LVLM inference, thereby enhancing computational efficiency. Extensive experiments across multiple models and benchmarks consistently demonstrate that V$^2$Drop maintains \textbf{94.0\%} and \textbf{98.6\%} of the original performance for image and video understanding tasks respectively, while reducing LLM generation latency by \textbf{31.5\%} and \textbf{74.2\%}.

organization_info.name: EPIC Lab, Shanghai Jiao Tong University

keyInsights: Visual tokens with high representational variation across LLM layers are consistently relevant to the task, providing a robust and spatially unbiased indicator of importance.
Leveraging intrinsic token variation for importance assessment eliminates the positional bias of attention-guided methods and ensures compatibility with efficient attention operators.
A theoretical framework connects the magnitude of inter-layer token variation to its potential impact on the model's final output, providing a principled basis for V²Drop.
