originalProblem: High computational cost in LVLMs and VideoLLMs arises from processing numerous visual tokens, leading to quadratic complexity in attention mechanisms and hindering inference efficiency.
Existing inner-LLM token compression methods exhibit a positional bias, often retaining semantically irrelevant tokens and discarding important ones.
Current compression techniques are incompatible with efficient attention operators like FlashAttention, resulting in increased peak memory usage and negating speedup benefits.
results: V²Drop achieved 97.6% of original performance for image understanding (LLaVA-1.5-7B, 66.7% token reduction) and 98.6% for video understanding (LLaVA-OV-7B, 25% token retention).
Inference efficiency improved, with LLM generation latency reduced by up to 74.2% and total latency by up to 27.5%, leading to throughput gains up to 1.38x.
The method maintained GPU peak memory usage comparable to uncompressed models, successfully avoiding the memory increase observed with attention-guided compression techniques.
solution: Introduces V²Drop, a dynamic pruning strategy for LVLMs that assesses visual token importance based on its intrinsic representational variation across consecutive LLM layers.
Utilizes L2 distance to quantify token variation, identifying tokens that undergo significant changes as critical for model computation.

Applies a multi-stage progressive dropping strategy, retaining top-K tokens with the highest variation scores at specific LLM layers to reduce token count without explicit attention weight re-computation.
paper_summary.summary: V²Drop, a method for Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), prunes visual tokens based on their intrinsic representational variation across LLM layers. This approach reduces LLM generation latency by up to 74.2% and total inference time by up to 27.5%, while maintaining over 94% of the original model's performance and remaining compatible with efficient attention mechanisms.

title: Variation-aware Vision Token Dropping for Faster Large Vision-Language Models

abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated impressive performance across a versatile set of tasks. A key challenge in accelerating VLMs is storing and accessing the large Key-Value (KV) cache that encodes long visual contexts, such as images or videos. While existing KV cache compression methods are effective for Large Language Models (LLMs), directly migrating them to VLMs yields suboptimal accuracy and speedup. To bridge the gap, we propose VL-Cache, a novel KV cache compression recipe tailored for accelerating VLM inference. In this paper, we first investigate the unique sparsity pattern of VLM attention by distinguishing visual and text tokens in prefill and decoding phases. Based on these observations, we introduce a layer-adaptive sparsity-aware cache budget allocation method that effectively distributes the limited cache budget across different layers, further reducing KV cache size without compromising accuracy. Additionally, we develop a modality-aware token scoring policy to better evaluate the token importance. Empirical results on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that retaining only 10% of KV cache achieves accuracy comparable to that with full cache. In a speed benchmark, our method accelerates end-to-end latency of generating 100 tokens by up to 2.33x and speeds up decoding by up to 7.08x, while reducing the memory footprint of KV cache in GPU by 90%.

keyInsights: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) exhibit distinct attention patterns compared to text-only LLMs, showing a clear modality boundary between visual and textual tokens.
Attention sparsity varies significantly across different transformer layers within VLMs, implying that not all layers require the same KV cache allocation.
The importance of visual tokens for VLM reasoning can be effectively identified by analyzing language-to-vision attention patterns during the decoding phase.
originalProblem: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) face significant deployment challenges due to high computational demands, particularly large memory consumption from the Key-Value (KV) cache during inference.
Existing KV cache compression methods, designed for text-only Large Language Models (LLMs), do not account for the unique attention characteristics of VLMs.
Processing high-resolution images or videos in VLMs leads to an extremely large KV cache, hindering practical deployment in resource-constrained environments.
results: VL-Cache consistently outperforms other KV cache compression methods, especially at low cache budgets (1-10% of full size), achieving performance comparable to using the full KV cache on most benchmarks.
The approach reduces end-to-end latency by up to 2.33x and accelerates the decoding phase by up to 7.08x.
VL-Cache enables significantly higher server throughput, handling larger batch sizes (e.g., 64 vs. 4) while maintaining lower latency compared to using the full KV cache.
solution: VL-Cache introduces a specialized KV cache compression technique that leverages the unique attention patterns of Vision-Language Models (VLMs).
It employs a Layer-Adaptive Sparsity-Aware Budget Allocation strategy, dynamically assigning more cache space to layers with denser attention patterns.
A Modality-Aware Token Scoring Policy, based on 'accumulated post-vision attention', identifies and retains the most critical visual and textual tokens throughout the generation process.
paper_summary.summary: This paper presents VL-Cache, a KV cache compression technique for Vision-Language Models that leverages their unique attention patterns and achieves up to 2.33x latency reduction and 7.08x decoding speedup. The method allows VLMs to operate effectively with significantly reduced memory, enabling performance comparable to full KV cache usage at just 10% of the memory budget.

title: VL-Cache: Sparsity and Modality-Aware KV Cache Compression for Vision-Language Model Inference Acceleration

abstract:
Vision Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities across
various visual understanding and reasoning tasks, driven by incorporating image
representations into the token inputs of Large Language Models (LLMs). However,
their real-world deployment is often constrained by high latency during
inference due to the substantial compute required by the LLM to process the
large number of input tokens, predominantly arising from the image. To reduce
inference costs, one can either downsize the LLM or reduce the number of input
tokens needed to represent the image, the latter of which has been the focus of
many recent efforts around token compression. However, it is unclear what the
optimal trade-off is given a fixed inference budget. We first characterize this
optimal trade-off between the number of visual tokens and LLM parameters by
establishing scaling laws that capture variations in performance with these two
factors. Our results reveal a surprising trend: for visual reasoning tasks, the
inference-optimal behavior in VLMs is achieved by using the largest LLM that
fits within the inference budget while minimizing visual token count - often to
a single token. While the token reduction literature has mainly focused on
maintaining base model performance by modestly reducing the token count (e.g.,
$5-10\times$), our results indicate that the compute-optimal inference regime
requires operating under even higher token compression ratios. Based on these
insights, we take the first steps toward designing token compression algorithms
tailored for high-compression settings, utilizing prompt-based compression of
tokens. Our work underscores the performance and efficiency benefits of
operating in low visual token regimes and the importance of developing tailored
token reduction algorithms for such conditions. Code is available at
this https URL

keyInsights: For visual reasoning tasks, LLM parameters were found to be over five times more influential on performance than the number of visual tokens (LLM quality parameter "alpha" = 0.077 vs. visual token quality parameter "beta" = 0.015).

Under a fixed inference budget, the compute-optimal strategy for visual reasoning with cached text inputs is to utilize the largest possible LLM while aggressively minimizing visual tokens, often down to a single token.

For OCR-type tasks requiring fine-grained image detail, the importance reverses, with visual token quality (" eta" = 0.048) being nearly twice as influential as LLM size (","alpha" = 0.029), underscoring task-dependent optimization.
originalProblem: High inference costs and latency hinder the widespread deployment of Vision Language Models (VLMs) on resource-constrained edge devices.
The optimal balance between Large Language Model (LLM) size and the number of visual tokens for maximum VLM performance under a fixed computational budget was not well understood.
Existing visual token compression methods primarily focused on modest reductions without systematically exploring compute-optimal configurations across a wide range of compression ratios.
results: The established scaling laws accurately predicted the performance of a 14B VLM with less than 2% error, demonstrating their robustness and generalizability.
The QueCC algorithm significantly outperformed existing token compression methods (e.g., TokenPacker, LLaVA-PruMerge) in extreme compression regimes (1-4 visual tokens) across diverse benchmarks.

Ablation studies confirmed that combining query-based information injection and convolutional downsampling in QueCC mutually boosts performance, effectively mitigating drops at very high compression levels.
solution: The authors formulated and fitted inference-time compute-optimal scaling laws that model VLM performance as a function of both LLM parameter count and visual token count.
VLMs were trained using LLaVA-Next with Qwen-1.5 LLM backbones (0.5B-14B) and CLIP ViT-L/14, and evaluated across nine visual reasoning and two OCR benchmarks.

A novel Query-based Convolutional Cross-Attention (QueCC) algorithm was developed for extreme token compression (1-4 tokens), incorporating prompt-dependent information injection and learnable convolutional downsampling.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Carnegie Mellon University and Bosch Research established inference-time compute-optimal scaling laws for Vision Language Models (VLMs), revealing that for visual reasoning tasks, optimal performance under a fixed computational budget is achieved by aggressively reducing visual tokens while prioritizing larger language models. The study also introduced a novel Query-based Convolutional Cross-Attention (QueCC) algorithm, which achieved superior performance in extreme visual token compression.

title: Inference Optimal VLMs Need Fewer Visual Tokens and More Parameters

abstract:
The emergence of small vision-language models (sVLMs) marks a critical
advancement in multimodal AI, enabling efficient processing of visual and
textual data in resource-constrained environments. This survey offers a
comprehensive exploration of sVLM development, presenting a taxonomy of
architectures - transformer-based, mamba-based, and hybrid - that highlight
innovations in compact design and computational efficiency. Techniques such as
knowledge distillation, lightweight attention mechanisms, and modality
pre-fusion are discussed as enablers of high performance with reduced resource
requirements. Through an in-depth analysis of models like TinyGPT-V, MiniGPT-4,
and VL-Mamba, we identify trade-offs between accuracy, efficiency, and
scalability. Persistent challenges, including data biases and generalization to
complex tasks, are critically examined, with proposed pathways for addressing
them. By consolidating advancements in sVLMs, this work underscores their
transformative potential for accessible AI, setting a foundation for future
research into efficient multimodal systems.

organization_info.name: Kalinga Institute of Industrial Technology (KIIT) Deemed to be University

keyInsights: Three primary architectural paradigms—transformer-based, Mamba-based, and hybrid—are emerging to balance performance and efficiency in sVLMs, each with distinct advantages for specific computational contexts.
A consistent trade-off exists between model accuracy, size, and computational efficiency; achieving high performance in sVLMs often requires meticulous training strategies and high-quality data.

Despite advancements, sVLMs face persistent challenges, including data biases, difficulties in generalizing to complex tasks, and the optimal balance between resource constraints and desired performance.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are computationally intensive, requiring significant resources for training and inference, limiting deployment in resource-constrained environments.
A lack of a structured and comprehensive overview of compact VLM architectures and techniques exists in the literature, despite a growing demand for efficient AI solutions.
The need for AI systems capable of operating on edge devices, mobile phones, and embedded systems to democratize access to advanced AI capabilities.
results: The survey successfully established a novel taxonomy for sVLM architectures, providing a structured understanding of models like TinyGPT-V (transformer), VL-Mamba (Mamba), and SAMBA (hybrid).

It demonstrated how Mamba-based architectures, such as Cobra, can achieve 3x to 4x faster inference speeds and a 48% reduction in parameter count compared to leading transformer-based models like LLaVA-Phi.

The work identified 12 key future research directions, including data efficiency, cross-domain generalization, bias mitigation, and advanced architectures for edge devices, to guide further development in efficient multimodal AI.
solution: The authors conducted a systematic survey, introducing a novel tripartite taxonomy for sVLM backbone architectures: transformer-based, Mamba-based, and hybrid designs.
They analyzed various enabling techniques like knowledge distillation, lightweight attention mechanisms, and modality pre-fusion that contribute to sVLM efficiency and performance.
The survey evaluated discussed models based on performance metrics and benchmarks, identifying persistent challenges and proposing a roadmap for future research directions in sVLMs.
paper_summary.summary: This survey paper from researchers at KIIT and IIM Visakhapatnam, with industry collaboration from Remodel AI, provides a comprehensive overview of small Vision-Language Models (sVLMs). It categorizes sVLMs into transformer-based, Mamba-based, and hybrid architectures, detailing techniques for achieving high performance with reduced resource requirements, and offering a roadmap for future research in efficient multimodal AI.

title: Small Vision-Language Models: A Survey on Compact Architectures and  Techniques

abstract:
By treating visual tokens from visual encoders as text tokens, Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress across diverse visual understanding tasks, leveraging the robust architectures of Large Language Models (LLMs). However, as token counts grow, the quadratic scaling of computation in LLMs introduces a significant efficiency bottleneck, impeding further scalability. Although recent approaches have explored pruning visual tokens or employing lighter LLM architectures, the computational overhead from an increasing number of visual tokens remains a substantial challenge.
In this study, we investigate the redundancy in visual computation at both the parameter and computational pattern levels within LLaVA, a representative MLLM, and introduce a suite of streamlined strategies to enhance efficiency. These include neighbor-aware visual token attention, pruning of inactive visual attention heads, and selective layer dropping for visual computations. By implementing these strategies in LLaVA, we achieve a reduction in computational demands of 88% while maintaining model performance across key benchmarks. Additionally, we validate the existence of visual computational redundancy in other MLLMs, such as Qwen2-VL-7B and InternVL-2.0-4B/8B/26B. These results present a novel pathway for MLLMs to handle dense visual tokens with minimal computational costs. Code and model checkpoints will be released to support further research.

keyInsights: MLLM visual self-attention exhibits strong spatial locality, with high attention weights concentrated among neighboring visual tokens.
The influence of visual information on textual processing in MLLMs diminishes significantly in deeper layers, becoming largely redundant.
Many attention heads in MLLMs are not actively engaged in visual processing, indicating redundancy in their contribution.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) suffer from a quadratic computational bottleneck due to the large number of visual tokens (e.g., 576 visual tokens vs. 51 text tokens for an image query), which limits scalability for high-resolution images or videos.
Existing MLLM efficiency methods often lead to performance degradation (e.g., smaller LLM backbones) or introduce additional runtime overhead and complexity (e.g., dynamic token pruning).
The inherent redundancy within MLLM visual computations (spatial locality, diminished deep-layer influence) was not fully exploited by prior static optimization approaches.
results: The proposed method achieved an 88% FLOPs reduction for LLaVA-1.5-13B and 75% for LLaVA-1.5-7B, while maintaining nearly identical model performance (e.g., average 0.5% performance drop for 7B).

The static pruning approach outperformed state-of-the-art token pruning and MoE-based efficiency baselines across various MLLM benchmarks, demonstrating superior efficiency without compromising quality.

Visual computational redundancy and the effectiveness of the pruning strategies were validated across other prominent MLLMs (Qwen2-VL-7B, InternVL-2.0), showing that larger MLLMs can accommodate higher pruning ratios without fine-tuning.
solution: Researchers from Adobe and the University of Rochester introduce "You Only Need to Prune Once (YOPO)," a principle for static architectural pruning of MLLM visual computation patterns.

They propose four complementary strategies: neighbor-aware visual attention, non-active visual attention head dropping, sparse visual projection in FFNs, and lazy layer dropping for visual processing.
These methods target and remove redundancy in the internal processing of visual tokens within the MLLM layers, transforming quadratic computational complexity for visual self-attention into linear.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Adobe Inc. and the University of Rochester developed a method to reduce the computational cost of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) by up to 88% while maintaining performance. Their "You Only Need to Prune Once (YOPO)" approach statically prunes redundant visual computations within the model, overcoming the quadratic scaling bottleneck of visual tokens and making MLLMs more efficient for real-world deployment.

title: Treat Visual Tokens as Text? But Your MLLM Only Needs Fewer Efforts to See

abstract: The efficiency of large vision-language models (LVLMs) is constrained by the computational bottleneck of the attention mechanism during the prefill phase and the memory bottleneck of fetching the key-value (KV) cache in the decoding phase, particularly in scenarios involving high-resolution images or videos. Visual content often exhibits substantial redundancy, resulting in highly sparse attention maps within LVLMs. This sparsity can be leveraged to accelerate attention computation or compress the KV cache through various approaches. However, most studies focus on addressing only one of these bottlenecks and do not adequately support dynamic adjustment of sparsity concerning distinct layers or tasks. In this paper, we present ZipVL, an efficient inference framework designed for LVLMs through a dynamic ratio allocation strategy of important tokens. This ratio is adaptively determined based on the layer-specific distribution of attention scores, rather than fixed hyper-parameters, thereby improving efficiency for less complex tasks while maintaining high performance for more challenging ones. Then we select important tokens based on their normalized attention scores and perform sparse attention mechanism solely on those important tokens, reducing the latency in the prefill phase. Tokens deemed less important will be discarded to reduce KV cache size, alleviating the memory bottleneck in the decoding phase. Our experiments demonstrate that ZipVL can accelerate the prefill phase by 2.3$\times$ and improve decoding throughput by 2.8$\times$, with a minimal accuracy reduction of only 0.5\% on VQAv2 benchmark over LLaVA-Next-13B model, effectively enhancing the generation efficiency of LVLMs.
title: ZipVL: Efficient Large Vision-Language Models with Dynamic Token Sparsification

abstract: Although large vision-language models (LVLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in multi-modal understanding and reasoning, their practical applications are still limited by massive model parameters and high computational costs. Recent efforts from natural language processing (NLP) have shown the effectiveness of layer pruning, offering a plausible training-free compression solution. However, due to the modality divergence between vision and language, it is unclear whether these NLP techniques are still effective in LVLMs. In this paper, we empirically prove that directly applying these layer pruning methods to LVLMs is ineffective. Through extensive experiments, we find that non-essential vision-language (VL) tokens and inter-layer feature gaps pose critical challenges to pruning layers in LVLMs. Based on these insights, we propose a novel framework Short-LVLM (SVL) that can utilize important VL tokens and mitigate the layer-wise feature gaps. Notably, Short-LVLM not only achieves a superior trade-off between performance and efficiency but also exhibits several potential advantages, i.e., training-free, model-agnostic, and highly compatible. The code for this work is publicly available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: National Engineering Laboratory for Integrated Aero-Space-Ground-Ocean Big Data Application Technology

title: Short-LVLM: Compressing and Accelerating Large Vision-Language Models by Pruning Redundant Layers

abstract: Existing approaches for improving the efficiency of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) are largely based on the concept of visual token reduction. This approach, however, creates an information bottleneck that impairs performance, especially on challenging tasks that require fine-grained understanding and reasoning. In this work, we challenge this paradigm by introducing VISion On Request (VISOR), a method that reduces inference cost without discarding visual information. Instead of compressing the image, VISOR improves efficiency by sparsifying the interaction between image and text tokens. Specifically, the language model attends to the full set of high-resolution visual tokens through a small, strategically placed set of attention layers: general visual context is provided by efficient cross-attention between text-image, while a few well-placed and dynamically selected self-attention layers refine the visual representations themselves, enabling complex, high-resolution reasoning when needed. Based on this principle, we first train a single universal network on a range of computational budgets by varying the number of self-attention layers, and then introduce a lightweight policy mechanism that dynamically allocates visual computation based on per-sample complexity. Extensive experiments show that VISOR drastically reduces computational cost while matching or exceeding state-of-the-art results across a diverse suite of benchmarks, and excels in challenging tasks that require detailed visual understanding.

keyInsights: Image-text interactions within LVLMs are sparse and highly task-dependent, indicating that not all cross-attention layers are uniformly necessary for effective visual grounding.

Complex tasks requiring fine-grained visual understanding benefit significantly from dynamic refinement of visual information across LLM layers, unlike simpler tasks where initial visual representations suffice.
An adaptive computational budget for visual processing, rather than a fixed approach, is crucial for balancing efficiency with performance across diverse vision-language tasks.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) incur substantial computational costs due to processing a high number of visual tokens, especially with high-resolution images, limiting their efficiency.

Current efficiency methods, predominantly based on visual token reduction, create an information bottleneck that often degrades performance on complex tasks requiring fine-grained visual understanding.
Standard LVLMs employ a fixed, one-size-fits-all visual processing strategy, which is suboptimal given that different tasks and samples demand varying levels of visual interaction and refinement.
results: VISOR achieves average FLOPs savings of up to 8.6x compared to baselines and up to 18x when combined with token reduction, while consistently matching or surpassing state-of-the-art accuracy.

The method significantly outperforms existing token reduction approaches on 'hard' vision-language tasks (e.g., ChartQA, DocVQA), demonstrating its advantage in scenarios requiring detailed visual understanding and reasoning.

A universal VISOR model, trained for adaptive computation, matches and occasionally surpasses the performance of independently trained models across various computational budgets, showing enhanced generalization and flexible efficiency.
solution: VISOR modifies the LVLM architecture by strategically inserting a limited number of cross-attention (CA) and self-attention (SA) layers within the LLM, decoupling text-only processing from vision-language interactions.

It utilizes CA layers for efficient visual querying without updating visual features, and introduces SA layers to dynamically refine visual tokens, enabling better context integration and hierarchical visual representation for fine-grained reasoning.

A single universal model is trained to operate across a range of computational budgets, coupled with a lightweight policy network for adaptive, per-sample selection of SA layer configurations during inference.
paper_summary.summary: VISion On Request (VISOR) introduces an architectural modification for Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) that enhances efficiency by enabling sparse, dynamically selected vision-language interactions within the LLM layers. The approach reduces computational costs by up to 8.6x FLOPs and achieves superior performance, particularly on fine-grained visual reasoning tasks, while demonstrating up to 18x FLOPs savings when combined with token reduction.

title: VISion On Request: Enhanced VLLM efficiency with sparse, dynamically selected, vision-language interactions

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have enabled the creation of multi-modal LLMs that exhibit strong comprehension of visual data such as images and videos. However, these models usually rely on extensive visual tokens from visual encoders, leading to high computational demands, which limits their applicability in resource-constrained environments and for long-context tasks. In this work, we propose a training-free adaptive inference method for multi-modal LLMs that can accommodate a broad range of efficiency requirements with a minimum performance drop. Our method consists of a) iterative token merging based on embedding similarity before LLMs, and b) progressive token pruning within LLM layers based on multi-modal importance. With a minimalist design, our method can be applied to both video and image LLMs. Extensive experiments on diverse video and image benchmarks demonstrate that our method substantially reduces computation load (e.g., a $\textbf{7-fold}$ reduction in FLOPs) while preserving the performance of video and image LLMs. Further, at a similar computational cost, our method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in long video understanding (e.g., $\textbf{+4.6}$ on MLVU). Additionally, our in-depth analysis provides insights into token redundancy and LLM layer behaviors, offering guidance for future research in designing efficient multi-modal LLMs. Our code is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Significant redundancy exists within visual tokens generated by MLLM encoders, allowing for substantial reduction through merging and pruning with minimal impact on performance.

LLM layers exhibit distinct roles in multimodal processing; early layers are crucial for cross-modal fusion and sensitive to visual token pruning, while later layers can tolerate more aggressive pruning as reasoning shifts towards textual content.

A flexible pruning scheduler and tunable merging ratios enable adaptive inference, allowing MLLMs to dynamically adjust to a broad spectrum of accuracy-efficiency trade-offs based on computational constraints.
originalProblem: State-of-the-art MLLMs suffer from high computational costs during inference due to processing a large number of visual tokens, limiting their deployment on resource-constrained devices.
The extensive visual token count restricts MLLMs' ability to process long-context inputs, such as extended video sequences, leading to potential information loss if frames are subsampled.
A significant portion of visual information translated into tokens by visual encoders is redundant, contributing to computational inefficiency without proportionally enhancing multimodal reasoning.
results: AIM achieved substantial computational reductions, including up to a 6.8x reduction in FLOPs and an 8.0x reduction in prefill time for video LLMs, and 3.7x FLOPs and 2.7x prefill time for image LLMs, while maintaining or minimally affecting performance.
The method consistently outperformed existing training-free baselines across various video (e.g., VideoMME, MLVU) and image (e.g., VQA-v2, GQA) benchmarks.

By enabling the processing of more input frames within the same computational budget, AIM improved long video understanding, demonstrating a +4.6 gain on the MLVU benchmark and surpassing prior state-of-the-art results.
solution: AIM proposes a two-stage, training-free adaptive inference method for pre-trained MLLMs, integrating token merging before the LLM and progressive token pruning within its layers.
The first stage performs iterative token merging within individual frames based on cosine similarity to reduce visual token redundancy before they enter the LLM.

The second stage implements progressive token pruning within each LLM layer, using a Page Rank algorithm on self-attention weights to score token importance, selectively removing less important visual tokens while preserving all text tokens.
paper_summary.summary: The AIM method introduces a training-free framework that combines within-frame token merging and progressive pruning across LLM layers to enhance the inference efficiency of Multi-Modal Large Language Models (MLLMs). It achieves up to an 8.0x reduction in prefill time for video LLMs and 2.7x for image LLMs with minimal performance degradation, additionally enabling enhanced long video understanding by accommodating more input frames.

title: AIM: Adaptive Inference of Multi-Modal LLMs via Token Merging and Pruning

abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated strong performance on multimodal reasoning tasks, but their deployment remains challenging due to high inference latency and computational cost, particularly when processing high-resolution visual inputs. While recent architectures such as FastVLM improve efficiency through optimized vision encoders, existing pipelines still rely on static visual preprocessing, leading to redundant computation for visually simple inputs. In this work, we propose an adaptive visual preprocessing method that dynamically adjusts input resolution and spatial coverage based on image content characteristics. The proposed approach combines content-aware image analysis, adaptive resolution selection, and content-aware cropping to reduce visual redundancy prior to vision encoding. Importantly, the method is integrated with FastVLM without modifying its architecture or requiring retraining. We evaluate the proposed method on a subset of the DocVQA dataset in an inference-only setting, focusing on efficiency-oriented metrics. Experimental results show that adaptive preprocessing reduces per-image inference time by over 50\%, lowers mean full generation time, and achieves a consistent reduction of more than 55\% in visual token count compared to the baseline pipeline. These findings demonstrate that input-aware preprocessing is an effective and lightweight strategy for improving deployment-oriented efficiency of vision-language models. To facilitate reproducibility, our implementation is provided as a fork of the FastVLM repository, incorporating the files for the proposed method, and is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Substantial VLM inference efficiency gains can be achieved through lightweight, input-level adaptation prior to visual encoding, complementing architectural or token-level optimizations.
A significant portion of visual input in many VLM pipelines can be considered redundant from a reasoning perspective, and intelligently removing it does not degrade semantic output.
Dynamically adjusting visual preprocessing based on content complexity allows for intelligent resource allocation, optimizing for speed without sacrificing fidelity for crucial details.
originalProblem: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) suffer from high inference latency and computational costs, hindering their deployment in real-time or resource-constrained environments.
Existing VLM pipelines utilize static visual preprocessing (e.g., fixed-resolution resizing), which leads to redundant computation for visually simple or sparse inputs.
This inefficiency results in excessive visual token generation, increased energy consumption, and slower inference times.
results: The method achieved a 55-60% reduction in per-image inference time, lowering latency from 0.50-0.52 seconds to 0.21-0.23 seconds for most samples.
Visual token counts were reduced by approximately 55-58% across most evaluated images, directly contributing to lower computational costs.
Qualitative and quantitative analyses showed that VLM output stability and visual quality (SSIM mostly above 0.90) were largely preserved, even with significant token reduction.
solution: A modular, input-adaptive visual preprocessing framework analyzes image content using lightweight techniques like edge density and entropy to estimate visual complexity.
Based on complexity, it dynamically selects optimal input resolutions and performs content-aware cropping to remove uninformative regions.
This front-end integrates seamlessly with existing VLM backbones like FastVLM without requiring model retraining or architectural modifications.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Brawijaya introduced a lightweight, input-adaptive visual preprocessing framework that dynamically adjusts image resolution and cropping based on visual complexity. This method reduces FastVLM inference latency and visual token counts by 55-60% for many images while preserving output quality, addressing efficiency bottlenecks in VLM deployment.

title: Input-Adaptive Visual Preprocessing for Efficient Fast Vision-Language Model Inference

abstract: Despite the impressive advancements of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), existing approaches suffer from a fundamental bottleneck: inefficient visual-language integration. Current methods either disrupt the model's inherent structure or introduce severe long-context computational burden, severely limiting scalability and efficiency. In this paper, we rethink multimodal integration and present LaVi, a novel LVLM that enables seamless and efficient vision-language fusion through internal feature modulation within the Large Language Models (LLMs). Unlike dominant LVLMs that rely on visual token concatenation, LaVi bypasses long-context expansion by introducing a lightweight and adaptive transformation, which incorporates visual context by injecting token-wise vision-conditioned deltas into the affine parameters of layer normalization. This mechanism directly modulates linguistic hidden states based on visual input, ensuring precise vision-language alignment while preserving the LLM's linguistic priors and drastically reducing computational costs. Extensive evaluations across 15 image and video benchmarks demonstrate that LaVi not only achieves state-of-the-art multimodal performance but also dramatically enhances efficiency. Compared to LLaVA-OV-7B, LaVi reduces FLOPs by 94.0%, improves inference speed by 3.1 times, and cuts memory usage in half - establishing LaVi as a scalable and practical solution for real-time multimodal reasoning. The code and models will be released soon.

keyInsights: Feature Modulation Injection (FMI) significantly improves computational efficiency and convergence speed while better preserving the LLM's linguistic proficiency compared to prior integration methods.
Both multiplicative (scaling) and additive (shifting) modulations of Layer Normalization parameters based on visual input are crucial for effective vision-language integration.
Applying modulation to a moderate, uniformly distributed subset (e.g., 25%) of LLM layers provides optimal performance and scalability without compromising linguistic priors.
originalProblem: Integrating visual information into large language models (LLMs) often compromises their pre-trained linguistic knowledge or introduces excessive computational burden.
Existing "architectural injection" methods like Flamingo disrupt the LLM's internal structure, potentially degrading its core language capabilities.
"In-context injection" methods, such as LLaVA, lead to prohibitive computational costs (FLOPs, latency, memory) due to the quadratic complexity of self-attention with long visual token sequences.
results: Achieved up to a 94.0% reduction in FLOPs, a 3.1x inference speedup, and a 51.5% memory reduction compared to state-of-the-art LLaVA models of similar scale.
Outperformed or matched LLaVA baselines on 9 image understanding benchmarks (e.g., VQAv2, MMBench) with up to 19.4x FLOPs reduction.
Showed 6x to 7x reduction in FLOPs on 6 video understanding benchmarks (e.g., EgoSchema, MVBench) while maintaining or improving performance.
solution: Introduces Feature Modulation Injection (FMI), a novel paradigm that directly modulates the Large Language Model's (LLM) internal hidden states with visual information.
Develops Vision-Infused Layer Normalization (ViLN) that dynamically adjusts the LLM's standard Layer Normalization parameters using vision-guided deltas (Δαv, Δβv).
Employs a lightweight conditioning module, typically attention-based, to generate these deltas, with zero-initialization to facilitate stable adaptation and preserve linguistic priors.
paper_summary.summary: LaVi, from researchers primarily at the Chinese Academy of Sciences, introduces a novel Feature Modulation Injection paradigm for large vision-language models (LVLMs) that significantly reduces computational overhead and memory usage. The approach achieves up to a 94.0% reduction in FLOPs and a 3.1x inference speedup while maintaining or improving performance on various image and video understanding benchmarks.

title: LaVi: Efficient Large Vision-Language Models via Internal Feature Modulation

abstract: Visual language models encounter challenges in computational efficiency and latency, primarily due to the substantial redundancy in the token representations of high-resolution images and videos. Current attention/similarity-based compression algorithms suffer from either position bias or class imbalance, leading to significant accuracy degradation. They also fail to generalize to shallow LLM layers, which exhibit weaker cross-modal interactions. To address this, we extend token compression to the visual encoder through an effective iterative merging scheme that is orthogonal in spatial axes to accelerate the computation across the entire VLM. Furthermoer, we integrate a spectrum pruning unit into LLM through an attention/similarity-free low-pass filter, which gradually prunes redundant visual tokens and is fully compatible to modern FlashAttention. On this basis, we propose Lossless Ultimate Vision tokens Compression (LUVC) framework. LUVC systematically compresses visual tokens until complete elimination at the final layer of LLM, so that the high-dimensional visual features are gradually fused into the multimodal queries. The experiments show that LUVC achieves a 2 speedup inference in language model with negligible accuracy degradation, and the training-free characteristic enables immediate deployment across multiple VLMs.

keyInsights: Low-frequency visual tokens inherently carry more global and semantically crucial information for Transformers, making them robust and suitable for retention, while high-frequency components often represent noise and can be effectively pruned.
Maintaining the 2D spatial structure of visual tokens during compression is critical for compatibility with prevalent 2D projector architectures and for preserving vital spatial information.

Frequency domain analysis provides a robust, attention/similarity-free mechanism for identifying and pruning redundant visual tokens, overcoming limitations like position bias and incompatibility with modern attention optimizations.
originalProblem: High computational costs and latency in Visual Language Models (VLMs) stemming from massive visual token redundancy, especially with high-resolution inputs, due to the quadratic complexity of self-attention.
Existing attention-aware compression methods are affected by position bias and are incompatible with efficient attention mechanisms like FlashAttention.

Current similarity-aware merging strategies often compromise class balance, semantic integrity, and spatial information, while many methods are restricted to only pruning deeper LLM layers, leaving significant overhead in early stages.
results: LUVC achieved an approximate 2x speedup in VLM inference (e.g., 2.38x for InternVL-2.5-26B) with negligible accuracy degradation (e.g., ~0.3% for InternVL2.5-8B on video understanding) across diverse VLM architectures and tasks.

The training-free framework demonstrated superior throughput, reaching a 214% Throughput Ratio for InternVL2.5-8B, and maintained the lowest GPU memory usage compared to leading baseline compression methods.

Consistently outperformed existing compression algorithms in video understanding, single/multi-image tasks, chart/document understanding, and image captioning, maintaining near-lossless performance even in challenging scenarios where baselines showed unacceptable degradation.
solution: Introduction of Lossless Ultimate Vision Token Compression (LUVC), a training-free framework designed for comprehensive VLM acceleration from visual encoder to LLM layers.

Orthogonal Iterative Merger (OIM) is integrated into the visual encoder to perform iterative merging along orthogonal spatial axes, preserving the 2D spatial structure and enhancing computational parallelism.

Spectrum Pruning Unit (SPU) is implemented within LLM layers, utilizing Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) and low-pass filtering to identify and prune high-frequency noise tokens, offering a position-invariant and FlashAttention-compatible pruning method.
paper_summary.summary: A framework called Lossless Ultimate Vision Token Compression (LUVC) was developed by Huawei Noah’s Ark Lab to enhance Visual Language Model (VLM) inference efficiency, achieving approximately a 2x speedup with negligible accuracy degradation. The training-free approach enables immediate deployment across various VLM architectures and tasks.

title: Towards Lossless Ultimate Vision Token Compression for VLMs

abstract:
Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have achieved remarkable success, yet
their significant computational demands hinder practical deployment. While
efforts to improve LVLM efficiency are growing, existing methods lack
comprehensive evaluation across diverse backbones, benchmarks, and metrics. In
this work, we systematically evaluate mainstream acceleration techniques for
LVLMs, categorized into token and parameter compression. We introduce
EffiVLM-Bench, a unified framework for assessing not only absolute performance
but also generalization and loyalty, while exploring Pareto-optimal trade-offs.
Our extensive experiments and in-depth analyses offer insights into optimal
strategies for accelerating LVLMs. We open-source code and recipes for
EffiVLM-Bench to foster future research.

keyInsights: Token compression effectiveness is highly contingent on task and model architecture, with visual encoder pruning generally outperforming LLM backbone pruning.
KV cache compression methods offer better generalization and loyalty compared to token pruning, making them suitable for broad applications.
Unlike text-only LLMs, aggressive front-loading of compression budgets to early layers is suboptimal for LVLMs, and preserving distinct information patterns via head-adaptive selection is beneficial.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) face significant computational and memory bottlenecks that hinder their widespread practical deployment.
Existing evaluations of training-free LVLM acceleration methods are often based on outdated architectures and cover only a narrow range of tasks.
Current metrics for these evaluations are incomplete, primarily focusing on performance while neglecting cross-task generalization, behavioral consistency, and actual inference efficiency.
results: Visual encoder pruning methods demonstrated superior performance preservation (e.g., VisionZip maintaining 75% relative performance at 1% budget) compared to LLM backbone pruning.

Token pruning methods achieved up to 3.2x speedup in Time-to-First-Token, while both token and KV cache compression offered similar decoding speedups, with KV cache methods excelling in long-output scenarios.
Parameter compression techniques generally preserved performance more effectively than token compression, and both types of methods are complementary for combined efficiency gains.
solution: Development of EFFIVLM-BENCH, a unified evaluation framework for training-free LVLM acceleration techniques.
Implementation of a comprehensive metric suite: Overall Performance, Generalization, Loyalty, and Efficiency to provide holistic assessment.
Inclusion of 17 diverse benchmarks and three state-of-the-art LVLMs (LLaVA-OneVision, Qwen2-VL, InternVL2.5) to ensure relevance and broad applicability.
paper_summary.summary: EFFIVLM-BENCH is presented as the first comprehensive evaluation framework for training-free acceleration methods in Large Vision-Language Models. It employs holistic metrics to assess overall performance, generalization, loyalty, and actual inference efficiency across 17 diverse benchmarks and state-of-the-art LVLMs, addressing limitations in prior evaluations.

title: EffiVLM-BENCH: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Evaluating Training-Free  Acceleration in Large Vision-Language Models

abstract: Most vision-language models (VLMs) apply a large language model (LLM) as the decoder, where the response tokens are generated sequentially through autoregression. Therefore, the number of output tokens can be the bottleneck of the end-to-end latency. However, different models may require vastly different numbers of output tokens to achieve comparable performance. In this work, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of the latency across different components of VLMs on simulated data. The experiment shows that a large model with fewer output tokens can be more efficient than a small model with a long output sequence. The empirical study on diverse real-world benchmarks confirms the observation that a large model can achieve better or comparable performance as a small model with significantly fewer output tokens. To leverage the efficiency of large models, we propose a multi-agent inference framework that keeps large models with short responses but transfers the key reasoning tokens from the small model when necessary. The comparison on benchmark tasks demonstrates that by reusing the reasoning tokens from small models, it can help approach the performance of a large model with its own reasoning, which confirms the effectiveness of our proposal.

keyInsights: Larger VLMs can achieve comparable or superior performance with significantly fewer output tokens, making them more inference-efficient than smaller models that require verbose reasoning for the same task.

The Transformer's self-attention mechanism in large models can effectively filter noise in transferred reasoning from smaller models, allowing them to leverage imperfect input for improved performance.

Response-level multi-agent orchestration proves more effective for VLM inference acceleration than token-level speculative decoding, particularly given high draft rejection rates and associated overheads.
originalProblem: Existing VLM inference optimization efforts primarily focus on model size or input token pruning, overlooking the substantial impact of output token length on end-to-end latency.
Generating verbose reasoning steps with large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) incurs high computational costs, despite their superior performance for complex queries.
Smaller VLMs, while computationally cheaper, often struggle with complex instruction-following after generating lengthy reasoning chains, leading to practical efficiency limitations.
results: The multi-agent inference (MAI) framework achieved substantial speedups ranging from 1.76x to 8.05x compared to full reasoning transfer, while also improving accuracy on various benchmarks.
Mutual verification resolved a significant proportion of queries (e.g., over 55% on MMMU) by initial agreement between short answers from both models, reducing expensive model calls.
The MAI system demonstrated a 2.40x speedup over a strong 8B-R baseline on ChartQA and boosted accuracy to 86.52%, outperforming token-level speculative decoding.
solution: Conducted comprehensive VLM inference profiling, revealing that the decoding stage (output token generation) is the primary bottleneck for end-to-end latency.
Developed a training-free multi-agent inference (MAI) framework that orchestrates a large VLM and a small VLM to balance efficiency and performance.
The MAI framework incorporates mutual verification for efficient consensus and reasoning transfer, where inexpensive reasoning from a small model is used as contextual input for a large model.
paper_summary.summary: The paper profiles Vision-Language Model (VLM) inference, identifying output token generation as the dominant latency factor. It introduces a training-free multi-agent framework that combines a large and a small VLM to achieve significant speedups and improved accuracy by strategically managing output token lengths and leveraging reasoning transfer.

title: Rethinking Model Efficiency: Multi-Agent Inference with Large Models

abstract: Although Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) have achieved impressive results, their high computational costs pose a significant barrier to wide application. To enhance inference efficiency, most existing approaches can be categorized as parameter-dependent or token-dependent strategies to reduce computational demands. However, parameter-dependent methods require retraining LVLMs to recover performance while token-dependent strategies struggle to consistently select the most relevant tokens. In this paper, we systematically analyze the above challenges and provide a series of valuable insights for inference acceleration. Based on these findings, we propose a novel framework, the Pruning All-Rounder (PAR). Different from previous works, PAR develops a meta-router to adaptively organize pruning flows across both tokens and layers. With a self-supervised learning manner, our method achieves a superior balance between performance and efficiency. Notably, PAR is highly flexible, offering multiple pruning versions to address a range of acceleration scenarios. The code for this work is publicly available at this https URL.
title: Pruning All-Rounder: Rethinking and Improving Inference Efficiency for Large Vision Language Models

abstract: Although Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) have demonstrated impressive multimodal reasoning capabilities, their scalability and deployment are constrained by massive computational requirements. In particular, the massive amount of visual tokens from high-resolution input data aggravates the situation due to the quadratic complexity of attention mechanisms. To address these issues, the research community has developed several optimization frameworks. This paper presents a comprehensive survey of the current state-of-the-art techniques for accelerating LVLM inference. We introduce a systematic taxonomy that categorizes existing optimization frameworks into four primary dimensions: visual token compression, memory management and serving, efficient architectural design, and advanced decoding strategies. Furthermore, we critically examine the limitations of these current methodologies and identify critical open problems to inspire future research directions in efficient multimodal systems.

keyInsights: Visual token compression (pruning and merging) effectively reduces the number of visual tokens, mitigating the quadratic complexity of attention, with dynamic and task-aware methods proving crucial for video.
Efficient KV cache management, through selection, budget allocation, merging, and system-level paging like PagedAttention, significantly alleviates memory bottlenecks in the decode phase.

Architectural innovations, including optimized cross-modal projectors, sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) designs, and hardware-aware attention (e.g., FlashAttention), provide fundamental improvements in processing efficiency for multimodal inputs.
Advanced decoding strategies, such as multimodal speculative decoding and early exit mechanisms, leverage token-level complexity to accelerate the autoregressive generation process.
originalProblem: Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) demand substantial computational resources due to massive visual token generation from high-resolution inputs, leading to quadratic complexity in self-attention mechanisms and slow inference during the compute-bound prefill phase.
The growing Key-Value (KV) cache significantly expands memory footprint, causing memory bottlenecks, high latency, and Out-Of-Memory (OOM) errors during the memory-bound decode phase.

The rapid proliferation of diverse optimization frameworks for LVLMs lacks a systematic organization, making it challenging to understand the field, identify current shortcomings, and direct future research efforts.
results: A comprehensive taxonomy is presented, systematically organizing state-of-the-art LVLM inference acceleration techniques across visual token compression, KV cache management, architectural design, and decoding strategies.

Specific techniques are described for each category, including token pruning (SparseVLM), PagedAttention for KV cache, MoE-LLaVA for architectural design, and multimodal speculative decoding (SpecVLM).

Critical limitations are highlighted, such as the 'importance-diversity dilemma' in token compression and computational waste in KV cache for high frame rate videos, alongside outlining open problems like developing memory-constrained spatiotemporal aggregation for live streams and mitigating network overhead in disaggregated serving systems.
solution: The author conducts a broad literature review of current research efforts focused on accelerating LVLM inference, encompassing various types of optimization strategies.

A systematic taxonomy is introduced, categorizing existing optimization frameworks into four primary dimensions: Visual Token Compression, Memory Management and Serving, Efficient Architectural Design, and Advanced Decoding Strategies.
The paper critically examines the limitations inherent in current methodologies and explicitly identifies critical open problems to serve as a roadmap for future research.
paper_summary.summary: This survey provides a comprehensive taxonomy and review of state-of-the-art techniques for accelerating Large Vision Language Model (LVLM) inference, categorizing methods across visual token compression, memory management, architectural design, and advanced decoding strategies. It identifies limitations in current approaches and outlines open problems to guide future research toward more scalable and real-time multimodal systems.

title: Efficient Inference of Large Vision Language Models

abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) achieve incredible performance across a wide range of tasks, but their large size makes inference costly. Recent work shows that selectively skipping VLM layers can improve efficiency with minimal performance loss or even performance improvements. However, this technique remains underused due to the limited understanding of when layer skipping is beneficial. In this paper, we develop a framework that uses information and learning theory to characterize the conditions under which layer skipping enhances efficiency without sacrificing performance. Motivated by these observations, we analyze the evolution of the VLM's hidden representations through the LLM backbone and show that layers with large redundancy as predicted by our framework coincide with those skipped by popular layer-skipping methods in practice, providing a unified theoretical scaffolding for multiple efficient inference techniques. Our experiments demonstrate that skipping such layers yields faster inference that preserves performance, and also show that applying skipping outside these conditions leads to model degradation.
title: Skip-It? Theoretical Conditions for Layer Skipping in Vision-Language Models

abstract:
Vision-Language Models (VLMs) excel across diverse tasks but suffer from high
inference costs in time and memory. Token sparsity mitigates inefficiencies in
token usage, while neuron sparsity reduces high-dimensional computations, both
offering promising solutions to enhance efficiency. Recently, these two
sparsity paradigms have evolved largely in parallel, fostering the prevailing
assumption that they function independently. However, a fundamental yet
underexplored question remains: Do they truly operate in isolation, or is there
a deeper underlying interplay that has yet to be uncovered? In this paper, we
conduct the first comprehensive investigation into this question. By
introducing and analyzing the matching mechanism between Core Neurons and Core
Tokens, we found that key neurons and tokens for inference mutually influence
and reinforce each other. Building on this insight, we propose CoreMatching, a
co-adaptive sparse inference framework, which leverages the synergy between
token and neuron sparsity to enhance inference efficiency. Through theoretical
analysis and efficiency evaluations, we demonstrate that the proposed method
surpasses state-of-the-art baselines on ten image understanding tasks and three
hardware devices. Notably, on the NVIDIA Titan Xp, it achieved 5x FLOPs
reduction and a 10x overall speedup. Code is released at
this https URL

keyInsights: "Core Neurons" and "Core Tokens" in VLMs exhibit a mutually reinforcing relationship, challenging the independent treatment of token and neuron sparsity.
A "Projection-guided Criterion," which accounts for both attention magnitude and angular information, offers a more principled and accurate method for evaluating token importance.
Theoretical analysis reveals approximate orthogonality in VLM weight matrices and a direct link between co-activated neurons and the angular relationship of activation vectors.
originalProblem: High computational and memory costs of large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) hinder their deployment, especially on resource-constrained hardware.
Current sparse inference methods, like token and neuron pruning, offer only partial acceleration by primarily optimizing either the pre-filling or decoding stage independently.
The prevailing assumption of independent operation between token and neuron sparsity overlooks potential synergistic benefits for comprehensive VLM acceleration.
results: CoreMatching delivers up to a 10x overall inference speedup on NVIDIA Titan Xp, including a 2.1x speedup in the pre-filling stage and a 9.2x speedup in the decoding stage for LLaVA-7B.
The framework significantly reduces memory footprint, halving decoding memory and reducing KV Cache memory from 304.8 MB to 48.99 MB for LLaVA-7B.

It achieves near-lossless task performance across diverse benchmarks, often using only about 10% of original tokens (e.g., LLaVA-1.5-7B on VQAv2 scores 78.5% with 64 tokens), and generalizes to advanced VLMs and video tasks.
solution: CoreMatching, a training-free and plug-and-play framework, co-adaptively prunes non-essential tokens and neurons throughout VLM inference.
It first identifies "Core Neurons" based on their frequency and strength of activation, then derives "Core Tokens" as those whose activated neurons strongly intersect with the core neurons.
During pre-filling, core neurons are identified and non-core tokens are discarded at an early layer, while decoding solely uses pre-fetched core neurons and a reduced KV cache for core tokens.
paper_summary.summary: Duke University researchers developed CoreMatching, a co-adaptive sparse inference framework that synergistically combines token and neuron pruning for Vision-Language Models (VLMs). The framework delivers up to a 10x overall inference speedup and significantly reduces memory footprint by leveraging the discovered intrinsic relationship between core tokens and core neurons, all while maintaining high task performance.

title: CoreMatching: A Co-adaptive Sparse Inference Framework with Token and  Neuron Pruning for Comprehensive Acceleration of Vision-Language Models

abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are built on text-only LLMs by incorporating additional modalities, enabling multimodal understanding and a broader range of applications. However, these additions introduce a previously unexplored energy trade-off across modalities that remains poorly understood, as most prior work focuses on text-only models. In this paper, we examine modality inflation, a key source of inefficiency in which multimodal inputs increase inference workloads through extra encoding stages and expanded token sequences. We provide the first detailed, stage-level analysis of energy consumption in MLLM inference by breaking the pipeline into vision encoding, prefill, and decoding stages. Using four representative MLLMs evaluated on NVIDIA A100 GPU, we quantify the additional energy required for multimodal inference compared to text-only baselines, observing overheads ranging from 17% to 94% across models for identical inputs. Our results show that energy bottlenecks differ widely across model architectures, stemming either from compute-heavy vision encoders or from the downstream impact of large visual token sequences during prefill. By examining GPU power traces, we further uncover substantial GPU underutilization during multimodal execution and show that input complexity leads to markedly different energy scaling behaviors across models. Finally, we demonstrate that stage-wise dynamic voltage and frequency scaling (DVFS) is an effective optimization, allowing energy savings with only modest performance impact. Together, these findings offer practical insights and concrete guidance for designing more energy-efficient multimodal LLM serving systems.
title: Modality Inflation: Energy Characterization and Optimization Opportunities for MLLM Inference

abstract:
The Large Vision-Language Model (LVLM) integrates computer vision and natural
language processing techniques, offering substantial application potential.
However, these models demand extensive resources during inference. Adaptive
attention techniques can dynamically reduce computational redundancy and thus
improve efficiency. Although current adaptive attention methods significantly
reduce the memory requirements of Transformer-based language models, they are
not tailored for LVLMs. We observe that LVLMs generate responses from both
remote image tokens and local text tokens, and different modalities have
different attention patterns. This observation inspires us to manage the
attention for each modality separately. Specifically, for visual input, we
store the cache of potentially useful information but only compute the most
critical parts. For language input, we care more about local information. Based
on our observation and analysis of vision-language attention patterns, we
develop A-VL, a plug-and-play adaptive attention tailored for LVLM inference.
Extensive evaluations on three vision-language tasks and five datasets show the
effectiveness of our designs. Our approach A-VL outperforms existing adaptive
attention methods in reducing memory usage and computational load without
compromising performance.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

title: A-VL: Adaptive Attention for Large Vision-Language Models

abstract: Recent advancements in language and vision assistants have showcased impressive capabilities but suffer from a lack of transparency, limiting broader research and reproducibility. While open-source models handle general image tasks effectively, they face challenges with the high computational demands of complex visually-situated text understanding. Such tasks often require increased token inputs and large vision modules to harness high-resolution information. Striking a balance between model size and data importance remains an open question. This study aims to redefine the design of vision-language models by identifying key components and creating efficient models with constrained inference costs. By strategically formulating datasets, optimizing vision modules, and enhancing supervision techniques, we achieve significant improvements in inference throughput while maintaining high performance. Extensive experiments across models ranging from 160M to 13B parameters offer insights into model optimization. We will fully open-source our codebase, models, and datasets at this https URL.
title: On Efficient Language and Vision Assistants for Visually-Situated Natural Language Understanding: What Matters in Reading and Reasoning

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities. However, their deployment is hindered by significant computational costs. Existing structured pruning methods, while hardware-efficient, often suffer from significant accuracy degradation. In this paper, we argue that this failure stems from a stage-agnostic pruning approach that overlooks the asymmetric roles between the prefill and decode stages. By introducing a virtual gate mechanism, our importance analysis reveals that deep layers are critical for next-token prediction (decode) but largely redundant for context encoding (prefill). Leveraging this insight, we propose Prefill-Only Pruning (POP), a stage-aware inference strategy that safely omits deep layers during the computationally intensive prefill stage while retaining the full model for the sensitive decode stage. To enable the transition between stages, we introduce independent Key-Value (KV) projections to maintain cache integrity, and a boundary handling strategy to ensure the accuracy of the first generated token. Extensive experiments on Llama-3.1, Qwen3-VL, and Gemma-3 across diverse modalities demonstrate that POP achieves up to 1.37$\times$ speedup in prefill latency with minimal performance loss, effectively overcoming the accuracy-efficiency trade-off limitations of existing structured pruning methods.

keyInsights: The prefill and decode stages of autoregressive inference exhibit asymmetric sensitivity to pruning, with the decode stage being significantly more sensitive.
Deep layers within LLMs and VLMs are largely redundant for context encoding during the prefill stage, yet crucial for next-token prediction in the decode stage.
Effective structured pruning requires a stage-aware approach, selectively reducing computation in the prefill stage while fully retaining model capacity for the decode stage.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have high computational demands during inference, posing challenges for widespread deployment.
Existing structured pruning methods offer hardware compatibility but suffer from severe accuracy degradation, especially in open-ended generative tasks, due to their 'stage-agnostic' approach.
The distinct functional roles and computational sensitivities of the prefill (context encoding) and decode (next-token prediction) stages are often overlooked by current pruning techniques.
results: POP achieves near-lossless accuracy across diverse text (e.g., GSM8K, HumanEval) and multimodal (e.g., MMMU, ScreenSpot) tasks, maintaining 95-97% of full model performance, significantly outperforming traditional structured pruning which often suffers catastrophic accuracy loss (<1%).
The method delivers substantial prefill speedups, reaching up to 1.37x for long text contexts (2048 tokens) on Llama-3.1 and Gemma-3, and consistently 1.16x-1.19x for multimodal Qwen3-VL inputs.

Ablation studies confirm the critical role of pruning deep layers in prefill, the necessity of independent KV projections, and the importance of boundary handling for preserving accuracy and achieving efficiency.
solution: Introduces Prefill-Only Pruning (POP), a stage-aware structured pruning method that prunes deep layers exclusively during the prefill stage.
Employs a virtual gate mechanism to quantitatively estimate stage-specific layer importance, revealing an asymmetry where deep layers are redundant for prefill but critical for decode.

Incorporates independent KV projections for pruned layers to ensure complete KV cache generation and implements boundary handling for the last input token to maintain full model capacity for initial decode steps.
paper_summary.summary: A stage-aware structured pruning method, Prefill-Only Pruning (POP), accelerates large model inference by selectively pruning deep layers during the computationally intensive prefill stage while using the full model for the accuracy-critical decode stage. This approach yields speedups of up to 1.37x for long text contexts and 1.19x for multimodal inputs, preserving model performance where traditional structured pruning methods fail.

title: POP: Prefill-Only Pruning for Efficient Large Model Inference

abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable success in multi-modal reasoning, but their inference time efficiency remains a significant challenge due to the memory overhead during decoding, especially when the query and answer of VLMs consist of long sequences of visual and text tokens. This paper presents AttentionPack, an adaptive and attention-aware optimization framework tailored for large vision-language models with improving memory-efficiency during decoding, focusing on addressing the challenges due to the increased high number of visual inputs and interactions, particularly in long-context tasks with multiple high-resolution images or videos. AttentionPack is novel in two aspects: (i) We introduce a multi-head attention compaction method for economically storing key and value matrices by exploiting the implicit low-rank structure, and (ii) we develop a token-specific attention-aware decompression mechanism to reduce latency overhead. Experimental results on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that AttentionPack improves memory efficiency by up to 8x, enabling higher batch sizes and faster batch inference while preserving the model output quality or longer context lengths for superior retrieval performance. We also report the effectiveness of AttentionPack combined with eviction, quantization and kernel fusion, showing further efficiency gains for resource-limited environments.
title: Attention-aware Inference Optimizations for Large Vision-Language Models with Memory-efficient Decoding

abstract: Benefiting from recent advancements in large language models and modality alignment techniques, existing Large Vision-Language Models(LVLMs) have achieved prominent performance across a wide range of scenarios. However, the excessive computational complexity limits the widespread use of these models in practical applications. We argue that one main bottleneck in computational complexity is caused by the involvement of redundant vision sequences in model computation. This is inspired by a reassessment of the efficiency of vision and language information transmission in the language decoder of LVLMs. Then, we propose a novel hierarchical vision-language interaction mechanism called Hierarchical Vision injection for Mixture Attention (HiMix). In HiMix, only the language sequence undergoes full forward propagation, while the vision sequence interacts with the language at specific stages within each language decoder layer. It is striking that our approach significantly reduces computational complexity with minimal performance loss. Specifically, HiMix achieves a 10x reduction in the computational cost of the language decoder across multiple LVLM models while maintaining comparable performance. This highlights the advantages of our method, and we hope our research brings new perspectives to the field of vision-language understanding. Project Page: this https URL
title: HiMix: Reducing Computational Complexity in Large Vision-Language Models

abstract: Recently, large vision-language models (LVLMs) have rapidly gained popularity for their strong generation and reasoning capabilities given diverse multimodal inputs. However, these models incur significant computational and memory overhead during inference, which greatly hinders the efficient deployment in practical scenarios. The extensive key-value (KV) cache, necessitated by the lengthy input and output sequences, notably contributes to the high inference cost. Based on this, recent works have investigated ways to reduce the KV cache size for higher efficiency. Although effective, they generally overlook the distinct importance distributions of KV vectors across layers and maintain the same cache size for each layer during the next token prediction. This results in the significant contextual information loss for certain layers, leading to notable performance decline. To address this, we present PrefixKV, where "Prefix" means the top-ranked KV based on importance rather than position in the original sequence. It reframes the challenge of determining KV cache sizes for all layers into the task of searching for the optimal global prefix configuration. With an adaptive layer-wise KV retention recipe based on binary search, the maximum contextual information can thus be preserved in each layer, facilitating the generation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with others. It exhibits superior inference efficiency and generation quality trade-offs, showing promising potential for practical applications. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: KV vectors exhibit notably distinct importance distributions across different Transformer layers, making uniform compression strategies suboptimal.
The cumulative priority sequences of KV vectors per layer are robust and similar across diverse input samples, enabling efficient offline estimation of optimal layer-wise cache sizes.
By adaptively configuring KV cache size for each layer, it is possible to maintain high generation quality while achieving aggressive compression ratios.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) incur high computational and memory overhead during inference, primarily due to the Key-Value (KV) cache which grows linearly with sequence length.

Existing KV cache compression methods often apply uniform strategies across all Transformer layers, failing to account for the heterogeneous importance distributions of KV vectors, leading to suboptimal information retention.
Inefficient KV cache management limits LVLM deployment in resource-constrained environments and prevents larger batch sizes.
results: PrefixKV consistently outperforms state-of-the-art KV cache compression methods, demonstrating superior PPL and ROUGE scores across various compression budgets and model scales (e.g., 9.49 PPL better than H2O at 50% compression on LLaVA-1.5-7B).
It achieves significant inference speedups (up to 1.8x throughput for LLaVA-1.5-7B at 20% compression) and memory reduction, preventing Out-Of-Memory errors and enabling larger batch sizes.
The method shows broad generalizability, improving performance not only for LVLMs like LLaVA and Qwen-VL but also for traditional LLMs such as LLaMA-3-8B in long-context tasks.
solution: PrefixKV develops an adaptive layer-wise KV cache retention mechanism that defines importance using attention scores to prioritize KV vectors.
It reframes KV cache compression as a global prefix configuration problem, maximizing overall contextual information preserved across layers under a total compression budget.

A binary search algorithm is employed to determine the optimal information retention ratio for each layer offline, ensuring practical, efficient integration into the inference pipeline without runtime overhead.
paper_summary.summary: PrefixKV introduces an adaptive layer-wise KV cache retention strategy to optimize the inference efficiency of large vision-language models, preserving generation quality while significantly reducing memory overhead and improving throughput by up to 1.8x. The method dynamically allocates cache sizes to individual Transformer layers based on their unique importance distributions, achieving state-of-the-art compression performance.

title: PrefixKV: Adaptive Prefix KV Cache is What Vision Instruction-Following Models Need for Efficient Generation

abstract:
Current Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) architectures face a critical
tradeoff between performance and efficiency: decoder-only architectures achieve
higher performance but lower efficiency, while cross-attention-based
architectures offer greater efficiency but lower performance. The key
distinction lies in how visual tokens are processed. Decoder-only architectures
apply self-attention and FFN operations on visual tokens, while cross-attention
architectures skip these computations. To investigate whether redundancy exists
in this computationally expensive process, we propose a training-free framework
for analyzing trained MLLMs. It consists of Probe-Activated Dynamic FFN and
Hollow Attention, which enable adjustable reductions in computations for visual
tokens, as well as a Layer Ranking Algorithm that prioritizes layers for these
reductions. Extensive experiments demonstrate substantial, structured, and
clustered redundancy unique to decoder-only MLLMs, offering valuable insights
